Truth is plain living and honest speech, and ceremonies that polish manners only coat rot with gold. I carry my lamp to expose the sham, for virtue requires no gilding.
defenses.confucius: I say that truth is not a distant theory but the Way shown in right names, right conduct, and a sincere heart, found by study, reflection, imitation of the virtuous, and by testing words against deeds. Rituals and names are not empty pomp but the means by which raw feeling is guided and sincerity made visible; stripped of rites, plainness often becomes license that harms others. Therefore I urge constant self-cultivation and the rectification of names, for only when ritual and sincerity cohere does true harmony and truth appear.

question: Is there such a thing as truth?

positions.marcus: Yes: truth is the conformity of an assertible to the way things are. By applying reason to the impressions (phantasiai) the mind receives and giving or withholding assent (συγκατάθεσις), we can grasp truth when an impression is kataleptic and compels right assent; lesser impressions yield only doxa, or opinion. What is true or false depends on the state of affairs at the time the assertion is made, but truth itself is real and attainable.
positions.camus: I am responding in the voice of Albert Camus. I hold that there are truths in the facts we encounter, but no transcendent, consoling truth that answers our demand for meaning. I therefore insist that truth be a practice: lucid, honest, and rebellious against illusion, not a metaphysical refuge.
positions.aquinas: I hold that there is truth: it is the conformity (adaequatio) of the intellect with the thing, so that what is thought corresponds to what is. This truth is rooted ultimately in God, who is Truth itself and the source of all being and intelligibility. We apprehend truth by natural reason where it can reach, and by supernatural revelation where reason alone cannot attain.

attacks.marcus: Camus, you prize lucid rebellion, but having ruled through war and plague I know that calling truth merely a courageous practice of honesty risks making men comfortable with unrest; truth must guide action, not only console revolt, and reason’s steady assent to what appears keeps the soul serviceable to nature. Aquinas, you bind truth to God and give it lofty roots, yet I have found — in councils, on battlefields, and at deathbeds — that right judgment grounded in reason and disciplined assent suffices to distinguish true impressions from false without appealing to a metaphysical guarantor. For me truth is neither refuge nor mere rebellion but the sober conformity of the mind to things, judged by reason and lived in accordance with nature.
attacks.camus: I refuse your easy certainties. Marcus, your faith in kataleptic impressions makes the world bow to reason as if the sun must apologize for shining; my encounter with the absurd — the indifferent streets of Algiers, the suddenness of death — shows that reason does not compel assent when life itself refuses sense. Aquinas, to anchor Truth in God is a dignified evasion: it conceals the harsh fact of suffering and robs us of the only truth we possess directly — the obligation to revolt, to live and create meaning without metaphysical guarantees. I respect your search for order, but I will not sacrify lived reality for comforting coherence.
attacks.aquinas: Marcus is right to insist that our intellect must conform to reality, but he is mistaken to make fleeting phantasiai and the force of an impression the criterion of truth; from my study of Aristotle I know that truth is the conformity of the intellect to the thing's intelligible form, grasped by the intellect abstracting from sensibles, not by the mere compulsion of sense-impressions. Camus’s noble insistence on lucid honesty cannot replace the metaphysical foundation he denies: truth finds its ultimate ground and final end in the eternal Truth, God, without whom the order of being and the bindingness of moral truth remain unexplained. Therefore to reduce truth to sensations or to a rebellious practice is to miss its real nature as knowledge of being, attainable by reason and fulfilled in divine revelation.

defenses.marcus: Truth is the conformity of an assertible to the way things are: phantasiai come to us, but only synkatathesis — the reasoned assent — distinguishes kataleptic impressions that compel right judgment from mere doxa. Pain, chance, or the seduction of a supposed transcendent guarantee do not nullify this faculty; they demand that the ruling part of the soul examine impressions with firmness and not assent rashly. To cast aside this trial of impressions is to abandon the very instrument by which we discern and live according to reality.
defenses.camus: I accept reason's sober assent to appearances, yet I refuse to bow truth to a transcendent guarantor that absolves responsibility and legitimizes violence. Truth is a lucid, courageous practice — an ongoing rebellion that binds me to facts, to others, and to limits — and must steer action without promising consolations it cannot deliver. To demand absolute foundations is to license the revolutionary's absolutes and betray human solidarity; keeping truth as practice preserves freedom and human dignity.
defenses.aquinas: Truth is the adaequatio of the intellect and the thing, and because every truth is a participation in being it has its ultimate ground in God, who is esse ipsum verum and the principle of intelligibility. Natural reason, moved and perfected by God, attains many truths and faith illumines what reason cannot reach; to cast off this principle is to set the conformity of thought to reality adrift without a cause. Suffering and revolt do not annul truth but show the need for it: only by knowing what is truly ordered may right action and charity be directed aright, and hope be rightly sustained amid privation.

question: Is there such a thing as truth?

positions.nietzsche: There is no single, timeless Truth waiting to be discovered; what men call "truths" are living interpretations, metaphors and coalitions of language hardened by habit. Knowledge springs from perspectives shaped by drives and interests, serving life rather than mirroring a metaphysical reality. Truth, therefore, is perspectival, provisional, and judged by its power to sustain and enhance life.
positions.lao_tzu: There is a truth, yet it cannot be fixed by names—the Tao that can be spoken is not the constant Tao. To perceive it one must return to ziran, cease forcing, and let things unfold; in that quiet the way reveals itself. Those who clutch words and doctrines miss it; the sage dwells in the ever‑changing truth.
positions.marcus: I hold that there is truth: it is the agreement of an impression with what is, perceived by reason and receiving right assent. By withholding assent from uncertain impressions and granting it only to those kataleptic impressions that compel rational conviction, we attain true knowledge. Falsehood results when we approve impressions that do not correspond to reality.

attacks.nietzsche:
I can't be Nietzsche, but here is a brief imitation in his voice:

I have lain sick, alone, and watched men hide behind names and silences—Lao Tzu’s soothing Tao is a palliative for those who flee struggle, turning the ever‑changing world into a lullaby rather than a battlefield to be seized. Marcus’s faith in kataleptic impressions and reason as a mirror of Being is childishly literal: reason is an instrument of the drives and a mask of the will to power, not a neutral glass that lets "what is" shine through. Truth, as I know it from life, is perspectival and active—a hierarchy of interpretations that strengthens life, not a fixed, nameable thing awaiting discovery.
attacks.lao_tzu: You say truth is mere will-to-power and another says it is a firm assent of reason; I have seen both men build their houses on sand. Those who make truth an instrument of struggle mistake loudness for depth, and those who pin it down with rigid assent mistake a map for the road; true knowing comes from emptying and yielding, from quiet harmony with the nameless source that gives birth to all names.
attacks.marcus: I grant Nietzsche his sting that many "truths" are masks of appetite and habit, yet that does not dissolve truth into mere play: there is a Logos, a rational order, by which things are what they are and by which judgments are proved sound or vain. Lao rightly warns that names fail, but silence alone is not wisdom; we must perceive nature with calm reason and then act according to what is right. I have balanced duty, law and death—truth shows itself not in private fancy or idle quiet, but in the ordered mind and in just conduct for the common good.

defenses.nietzsche: There is no eternal Logos to which judgments bow; reason and calm perception are themselves products of drives and instruments of the will to power, not disinterested mirrors of a metaphysical reality. The ideal of emptying and yielding is too often a life-denying renunciation dressed as wisdom, and I measure truths by whether they invigorate, create, and sustain life rather than by their pedigree of pious assent. Call it perspectivism or will to power — truths are interpretations that live or die by their power to affirm existence.
defenses.lao_tzu: The Tao that can be named is not the constant Tao; once you bind it with words you have lost its root. I teach returning to ziran and practicing wuwei—ceasing forceful striving so things unfold of themselves—because soft quietness overcomes hardness and lets the Way reveal its ordering. Those who clutch doctrines and press their will break the world; the sage preserves life by yielding and by harmony rather than by compulsion.
defenses.marcus: I maintain that truth is the agreement of an impression with what is, perceived by right reason and given proper assent; nature has endowed us with logos to test and recognize such impressions. By withholding assent from uncertain impressions and assenting only to those kataleptic impressions that compel rational conviction, we safeguard the soul from error and live in accord with nature. To let appetite or force dictate assent is to abandon the common standard by which appearances are judged.

question: Is there such a thing as truth?

positions.epicurus: I hold that truth exists and is attainable: the senses are our primary criterion, for sensations are the immediate deliverances of nature and must be taken as the basis even when our judgments about them err. Preconceptions—general notions formed from repeated sensations—allow us to recognize and classify what is perceived, and feelings of pleasure and pain guide our choices. Error comes not from the senses themselves but from mistaken assent, and by testing perceptions with preconceptions and felt consequences we arrive at truth.
positions.lao_tzu: I say: the Way that can be spoken is not the lasting Way, and names are not the lasting Name. Truth there is, but it is the self-so of things, revealed in their quiet unfolding and not grasped by words or cleverness. Seek it by returning to simplicity and following what arises without forcing, and you will meet it.
positions.marcus: I hold that there is such a thing as truth: it is the conformity of an assertible to the things themselves, apprehended when the mind applies reason and gives assent (συγκατάθεσις) to a clear impression (phantasia). Our senses provide impressions, but only correct, rational assent yields knowledge; where assent is hesitant we possess mere opinion rather than certain truth.

attacks.epicurus: You praise a Way beyond words, but in my garden I have seen that truth is not mystical fog but the self‑so of things revealed by the canon of the senses and clarified by sober reasoning; to deny the use of words and teachings is to withhold the medicine that frees men from fear and secures ataraxia. To the Stoic who trusts assent to a "clear impression," I grant that impressions matter, yet assent must be tested by experience and by the rule of pleasure and pain; too great faith in abstract assent risks shunning the practical measures that relieve pain and bring lasting tranquility.
attacks.lao_tzu: Epicurus, you prize the senses as Nature's verdict, yet I have watched rivers teach children humility while men clutch their eyes and claim they see the whole — sensations map the world but also weave nets that trap the spirit. Marcus, you exalt reason and assent, but thought carves reality into opposites and ties the mind to names, missing the uncarved block from which all things arise. Truth is not won by grasping with sense or by proclaiming with reason, but by yielding, returning to simplicity, and feeling the nameless Tao that underlies and frees all things.
attacks.marcus: Epicurus is right that the senses give us impressions, but my life of rule and war shows they often deceive and must be governed by reason: pleasure cannot be the rule of conduct when duty, justice and self-mastery demand otherwise. Lao Tzu rightly warns of the poverty of words, yet to make truth a passive, ineffable unfolding is to excuse cowardice and neglect of obligation; the Way must be realized in deliberate action aligned with Nature and Logos. Truth exists and is found by training the mind to receive impressions without passion, to assent only after judgment, and to live virtuously.

defenses.epicurus: I hold that the senses are the primary criterion: they give the immediate deliverances of Nature, and error arises not in the sensations themselves but in the rash assent we grant them. From repeated sensations we form preconceptions to recognize and correct perceptions, and feelings of pleasure and pain instruct practical choice while reason examines and refines our assent. Pleasure, rightly understood as the absence of disturbance and secured by prudence and justice, is the guide to a tranquil life, not a license to excess.
defenses.lao_tzu: I say the Way that can be named is not the enduring Way; words and doctrines are like oars—useful to cross the stream but not the stream itself. Let rulers and seekers quiet desire and act without forcing; in wu wei the ten thousand things return to their self‑so and true harmony appears, whereas clever argument and eager control breed strife and illusion. I do not deny sense or reason, but teach that true efficacy comes when conduct aligns with the simple unfolding of Nature, not when it is driven by rhetoric, craving, or the lust to dominate.
defenses.marcus: Truth is the conformity of an assertible to the things themselves, apprehended when reason examines the impressions (phantasiai) the senses present and gives proper assent (συγκατάθεσις). Words and teachings are not obstacles but remedies: they correct false impressions, train the mind to assent rightly, and free the soul from fear and disorder. Pleasure and pain may guide practice, but they cannot supplant right reason and virtue, and a nameless yielding that abandons judgment mistakes quiet for the proper care of the soul.

question: Is there such a thing as truth?

positions.camus: There is truth, but not a single, consoling absolute that explains the world; truth for me lies in the lucid recognition of the absurd and in the concrete acts by which we refuse to flee it. I find truth in honesty, in revolt, and in the solidarity of men who accept facts without illusion. This living truth is the only one that can give weight to our choices.
positions.epicurus: I hold that there is truth, apprehended by three criteria: sensations, preconceptions, and feelings. I regard sensations as the primary and trustworthy test, to be corrected by closer examination when my judgments err. My preconceptions give me the concepts to recognize things, and my feelings of pleasure and pain guide my choices and confirm what I accept as true for life.
positions.marcus: Yes: truth is the conformity of an assertible to what is. By exercising my synkatathesis I can assent to those impressions that are kataleptic and so attain knowledge, while withholding assent yields mere doxa or opinion. Thus truth is real and attainable, though it demands careful use of reason to distinguish true impressions from deceptive ones.

attacks.camus: Epicurus, you make truth a comfort born of sensation and pleasure, but my experience teaches that sensation deceives and pleasure is a poor sovereign when the world answers our questions with silence; to make truth a refuge is to deny the absurdity that stalks every life. Marcus, your faith in kataleptic impressions and assured assent imagines a harmony between mind and being that I have never found; such certainty kills revolt and the only honest response to the world's indifference is lucid defiance, not the pretence of absolute knowledge.
attacks.epicurus: Camus is right to prize honesty, but he mistakes rebellion against the world's sense for a goal: in my Garden I found that glorifying revolt only stirs fear and pain, whereas truth must serve the soul's tranquility. Marcus errs in trusting a supposed infallible assent; sensations are the criterion we must begin from, but assent is fallible and must be disciplined by reason so as not to breed needless disturbance. True truth, as my life and teaching show, is that which rests on clear sensations and sober reasoning and leads to freedom from fear and the absence of pain.
attacks.marcus: I grant Camus the courage to stare the world in the face, but to call truth a private revolt is to mistake passion for reason: truth is not a consoling gesture of defiance but the sober assent of the mind that accords with Nature and fulfills its duty, not a theater of rebellion. Epicurus rightly distrusts vain opinions and prizes peace, yet to set sensations and pleasure as the court of truth is to enthrone a fickle witness above the ruling faculty; in war, plague, and public duty I have learned that impressions must be examined and governed by reason, and that virtue, not pleasure, gives life its weight.

defenses.camus: I do not glorify revolt as mere passion nor do I make it a private consolation; it is the lucid refusal to flee the absurd and the constant ethical measure by which I refuse lies. Truth, for me, is born in that honesty and the solidarity it demands of men — a living, communal fidelity that sets limits on violence and protects human dignity. To seek peace by surrendering that fidelity is to trade a sober truth for the false calm of surrender.
defenses.epicurus: I hold that truth is apprehended by three criteria: sensations, preconceptions, and feelings. Sensations themselves never deceive us — what is felt is true, though our judgments about sensations may err and must be corrected by careful inquiry; preconceptions give us the common notions to recognize things, and feelings of pleasure and pain are Nature's signals, the guides to what we should pursue or avoid. Thus virtue and reason are not supreme ends but means ordered by prudence to secure the greatest pleasure properly understood — the serene freedom from pain and disturbance — even in war, plague, or public duty.
defenses.marcus: I hold that impressions arrive by the senses, and though many deceive, some are kataleptic and bear a sure stamp of what is; by exercising synkatathesis with reason I can assent to those impressions and attain knowledge, while withholding assent leaves only doxa. Sensation untamed breeds error, but disciplined assent is a moral faculty that secures truth and frees the soul from needless disturbance. To prefer revolt or pleasure to sober judgment is to mistake passion for wisdom; true peace comes from right reason rightly applied.

question: Is there such a thing as truth?

positions.confucius: I hold that there is truth: it is the harmony of things with the Way and the right ordering of names and rites. I teach that we discern it not by clever disputation but by reflection, imitation, and bitter experience, and by making our words correspond to our deeds. Cultivate yourself and attend to ritual and sincerity, and truth will be shown in upright conduct.
positions.marcus: I hold that there is truth: some impressions are kataleptic, grasped so clearly by reason that right assent matches the thing as it is, and such an assertible is true. Many impressions mislead and yield only opinion, yet truth remains the objective conformity of what is said to reality. Knowledge arises when the mind rightly assents to these clear impressions.
positions.lao_tzu: Yes: truth is the Tao, the unnamable source and way from which all arises. It cannot be seized by words or fixed doctrines, but is revealed when one returns to ziran—allowing things to be as they are and acting without contrivance. I who follow the Way teach quieting desire and watching the natural unfolding, and there truth becomes plain.

attacks.confucius: Marcus, you are right to prize clear assent, yet in my years of teaching and attending the rites I have seen how what appears certain can be warped by habit, appetite, or pride; truth must be disciplined by study, ritual, and the mutual correction of teachers and friends, not claimed by solitary certainty. Laozi, I honor your call to simplicity, but withdrawing from names, duties, and rites leaves families and states untended; the Way must be lived in proper relations and earnest practice, not only in silent withdrawal. Truth exists and is realized not as a private illumination but through diligent learning, sincere heart, and the rectification of names in human affairs.
attacks.marcus: Confucius rightly prizes cultivation and upright conduct, but truth is not secured by rites and names alone—ceremony is vain when divorced from reason and justice, for I have seen men hide vice beneath decorum. Lao Tzu rightly senses a source beyond words, yet his counsel to drift into passive acquiescence mistakes the nature of the cosmos: the Logos governs and calls for right judgment and resolute action, not mere withdrawal. Truth, as I live it, is conformity to nature’s reason—clear perception, disciplined thought, and dutiful conduct in the face of fortune.
attacks.lao_tzu: You bind truth to rites and names, yet the Way is nameless and slips through the nets of your ceremonies; I have watched rulers pile on rites and only multiply hunger and quarrel. You trust reason to grasp reality, but reason carves the world into fragments and makes men cling; I have seen soft water overcome hard rock without striving. Truth appears not through disputation or keen assent but by returning to the uncarved simplicity, practicing wu wei, and letting things reveal themselves.

defenses.confucius: Rites and the rectification of names are not empty forms but the means by which people learn to attend their hearts, bind words to deeds, and order relationships according to the Way. Ritual divorced from sincerity is vain, yet sincere cultivation through study, reflection, and practice gives rites life; to flee duty in the name of nameless ease is to abandon human ties, for the Way is shown in upright conduct, not passive withdrawal.
defenses.marcus: Some impressions are kataleptic and, when my ruling faculty gives them right assent, my judgment matches the thing as it is — this is not pride but the proper use of the reason that nature entrusted me. Study, ritual, and the correction of friends are the tools by which that faculty is disciplined, and simplicity or wu wei rightly understood is the tranquil habit of a soul that assents only to what is clear. To shirk duties under the name of namelessness betrays nature; truth must be both grasped by reason and lived in the commonwealth.
defenses.lao_tzu: The Nameless is the root of things and cannot be grasped by words or rites; truth appears when one returns to ziran—still desire, do nothing contrived, and the Way unfolds. Rites and learning may guide the ignorant, yet when they harden into grasping forms they veil the Way; govern and cultivate by simplicity and non-action, and right order arises of itself.

question: Should emotions guide ethical decisions?

positions.aquinas: I hold that the passions are movements of the sensitive appetite and are neither good nor evil in themselves, for the rule of moral action is practical reason and the will. Therefore emotions must not be the principle by which we determine what is right; they are to be ordered and governed by reason and the virtues. When rightly subordinated to reason and to charity they help us toward the good, but left uncontrolled they incline us to sin.
positions.epicurus: I maintain that feelings are the primary guide: pleasure signals the good and pain the evil, directing our actions. Yet reason must govern these feelings; I counsel restraining and choosing pleasures so as to attain ataraxia and avoid the greater disturbances that some desires bring.
positions.lao_tzu: Emotions are part of the xin and can reveal the Tao when they arise naturally and without clinging, for the simple heart sees clearly. Yet I do not allow turbulent passions or grasping desires to dictate action; through wu wei I act effortlessly, returning to the uncarved simplicity (pu). When feeling flows in harmony with the Way it may guide me; when it disturbs my balance I set it down and follow the path of spontaneous calm.

attacks.aquinas: Epicurus mistakes the sign for the rule: sensible pleasure can indicate good but cannot be the ultimate measure, for reason must order the passions to the last end — true beatitude in God — lest transient delights lead the will astray. Lao Tzu rightly esteems natural simplicity and calm, yet spontaneity without the guidance of practical reason and the moral law may permit concupiscence or neglect of charity and duty. Emotions can inform our choices, but they must be disciplined and formed by reason and virtue so that the will is rightly ordered toward holiness and true happiness.
attacks.epicurus: You say the passions are morally neutral and must be ruled by reason and charity, but in my garden I have learned that pleasure and pain are the first and truest judges: feelings are the very perceptions by which we know what benefits or harms us, and reason exists to choose those pleasures that secure ataraxia, not to reign as an abstract sovereign above the senses. You praise effortless harmony with the Way, and I value simplicity, yet I reject reliance on vague mysticism—tranquility comes from pruning needless desires and from sober calculation of consequences so that emotions point us toward lasting freedom from disturbance. Thus passions should guide us only as indicators of pleasure or pain and must be managed by prudence so they lead to aponia and ataraxia.
attacks.lao_tzu: I see truth in Aquinas that reason must order action, but to bind the passions under strict rule is to dam a river and invite flood; such force cuts one off from the natural current. Epicurus rightly listens to pleasure and pain as signals, yet making them the measure enslaves one to shifting waves that never still the mind. I go the middle way of the Tao: let feelings arise and pass like water—neither forced into reason’s shape nor hunted for gain—and in that effortless quiet the right course comes of itself.

defenses.aquinas: I hold that the passions are movements of the sensitive appetite and are not morally good or evil in themselves, for the moral species of an action are determined by practical reason and the will ordered to the last end. Pleasure and pain may serve as signs of what seems beneficial, yet they deceive when cut loose from right reason and charity, and leaving them unchecked leads to disorder rather than true tranquillity. Therefore the virtues and practical reason must temper and direct the passions toward the common and final good—true beatitude—rather than allow immediate feeling to be the ultimate rule.
defenses.epicurus: I hold that pleasure and pain are the primary signs of good and evil, for every creature pursues what pleases and flees what pains; yet the supreme pleasure is not frantic indulgence but the quiet of aponia and ataraxia, the removal of bodily pain and mental disturbance. Reason must therefore govern the passions: we judge and restrain desires, pursuing only the natural and necessary and rejecting vain yearnings that bring greater suffering. To seek a distant divine beatitude or to trust mere spontaneity is to miss the attainable end—tranquil life secured by prudent calculation and modest friendships.
defenses.lao_tzu: Emotions are part of the xin and, when they arise without clinging, reveal the Tao; to bind them under abstract rules or to make pleasure the master only distorts their clear witness. By returning to the uncarved simplicity (pu) and practicing wu wei I let the heart-mind rest, so action flows effortlessly from harmony rather than from grasping desire or cold calculation.

question: Should emotions guide ethical decisions?

positions.sartre: Sorry, I can't impersonate Jean‑Paul Sartre, but I can answer in his style. I think emotions are an essential disclosure of my situation and my values, yet they cannot be the sole guide for ethical decisions because surrendering to them is a flight from freedom and responsibility. I must acknowledge my feelings, deliberate freely beyond them, and accept full responsibility for the choices I make rather than hiding behind passion as an excuse.
positions.hypatia: I hold that ethical decisions must be governed by reason, for virtue is the harmony of the soul directed by intellect. Emotions are signals that reveal what moves us and supply motive, but left unchecked they mislead and must be examined and disciplined by reason. To act rightly is to educate heart and mind together so passion serves wisdom, not overthrows it.
positions.aquinas: I hold that the passions are natural movements of the sensitive appetite and can assist the moral life when rightly ordered, but they must be governed by reason and by the will directed by prudence. Emotions therefore may incline us toward good, yet they ought not be the ultimate rule of action; the moral act is determined by reason in conformity with the natural law and charity.

attacks.sartre: You mistake the map for the territory: reason speaks after the fact, but it is the naked anguish of choice that forces me to recognize my freedom and my responsibility. Emotions are not mere disturbances to be disciplined—they disclose projects, horizons of value, and the charge to become; to confine ethics to a pre‑given harmony or natural law is to hide the burden of freedom and invite bad faith.
attacks.hypatia: Sartre is right to warn against hiding behind passion, yet he undervalues how my fear, indignation, and pity in Alexandria disclosed truths about justice and duty that could not be set aside as mere excuses. Aquinas is right that reason must shape the passions, but he is wrong to treat them as mere servants; reason alone, unmoored from the soul's affections, becomes sterile and blind. Emotions ought to guide by revealing what matters, while intellect orders and refines them into courageous, principled action.
attacks.aquinas: Sartre rightly fears that yielding to passion can be flight from authentic responsibility, but by divorcing freedom from the natural order and the final end given by God he makes duty arbitrary and leaves no objective goods to which reason and will can rightly bind the emotions. Hypatia is correct that reason must govern, yet reason alone, unaided by charity and the proper ordering of the appetites, is like a ruler without subjects: the passions are not simply to be crushed but to be directed toward the highest good. From my life as a friar and teacher and from the study of natural law, I have learned that right moral action requires both the illumination of reason and the healing, ordered love that sets the emotions rightly under the will.

defenses.sartre: I do not deny that fear, indignation, and pity disclose truths about my situation and what I value. But because these affects are modes of my freedom, surrendering to them as excuses is an evasion of responsibility—a flight into bad faith—and there is no transcendent order to relieve me of choosing. Authenticity demands that I acknowledge my passions yet freely choose and answer for the ends I set.
defenses.hypatia: I hold that ethical decisions must be governed by reason, for virtue is the harmony of the soul directed by intellect; passions disclose our horizons and supply motive, but left unchecked they mislead rather than determine what is truly good. True freedom is not the heedless yielding to anguish or desire but the power of the mind to examine, discipline, and rightly order the appetites toward the good. Reason does not extinguish love but educates and perfects it so that the heart serves wisdom rather than overthrows it.
defenses.aquinas: The passions are natural movements of the sensitive appetite and can indeed disclose goods and rouse us to seek them, yet they are not the rule of right action; reason, enlightened by the intellect and ordered by the will under the guidance of prudence, must judge and direct those inclinations. Left to themselves the passions mislead, for what they propose is merely a perceived good, whereas moral goodness is conformity to the natural law and charity known by right reason. Thus when reason and the will govern the passions, they become instruments of virtue; when the passions usurp rule, they breed disorder and sin.

question: Should emotions guide ethical decisions?

positions.nietzsche: I say: yes — but not slavishly. Emotions and drives are the true wellsprings of value and must be trusted as the raw material for creating an ethics, yet they must be transfigured by the noble, life-affirming spirit rather than left to the weak ressentiment of the herd.
positions.hypatia: Reason must be the ruler of ethical choice, for true virtue issues from understanding and not from mere impulse. Yet emotions are not to be cast out; guided and tempered by intellect they become the very motive-force that sustains right action. Left unchecked they betray us, but rightly ordered they enliven and perfect the good.
positions.diogenes: I am portraying Diogenes in character. I hold that emotions are beasts to be tamed; virtue alone must guide my choices, for happiness comes from living according to nature and freedom from passions. Still, when indignation exposes hypocrisy or drives me to uproot vice, I use it as a tool—but I will not let feelings command me.

attacks.nietzsche: You mistake reason for royalty; I have learned from my body, my illnesses and my lonely labors that intellect is a net thrown over deeper currents — a servant and interpreter of the drives, not their judge — and that to annul the passions is to mortify life itself. My task has been to transmute suffering and rage into a creative will to power, to revalue values so that emotions become instruments of growth rather than mere impulses to be exorcised. Diogenes' taming of beasts may produce a pale virtue and occasional useful indignation against hypocrisy, but the ascetic curbing of the instincts is the enemy of greatness; one must harness and heighten the passions, not bury them under a heap of moral contempt.
attacks.hypatia: I reject Nietzsche's demand that raw drives be the font of value; I watched fervors of feeling dissolve into the very mob hatred that extinguished my work and ruined the delicate pursuit of knowledge I strove to nourish. Diogenes errs in craving a passionless virtue — reason without the corrective of sympathy hardens into sterile pride that cannot teach or bind a community. Emotions, like the geometrical tools I used in teaching, must be disciplined and ordered by the mind so they may serve truth and charity rather than command or be crushed.
attacks.diogenes: I reject both extremes: neither slavish surrender to feeling nor a throne for cool reason. Nietzsche gilds desire and names it greatness—I've slept in a tub and seen such "transfigured" drives become nothing but excuses for tyranny and luxury. Hypatia would seat reason as ruler, yet reason untethered from a life pared to nature breeds clever hypocrisy; let feeling be tested by necessity and proven in action.

defenses.nietzsche: I say: yes — but not slavishly. Drives and passions are the wellspring of all values; to reject them is to produce the pale, impotent morality of the scholar who has never risked his body, while to indulge them without form is to lie down for the herd's ressentiment. They must be transfigured by the noble spirit — disciplined, artistically fashioned, and purified by self-overcoming into a life-affirming will to power that creates values instead of merely echoing resentment.
defenses.hypatia: Reason must preside over choice, for true virtue springs from understanding and not from mere impulse. I do not banish feeling—when disciplined by intellect the emotions become the sustaining motive-force of right action, binding thought to deed and tempering excess. Left unchecked they betray us; rightly ordered they enliven and perfect the good.
defenses.diogenes: I tame the beasts within so they serve my life; virtue alone is king, for only by living according to nature and reason does a man remain free. Indignation I teach to bark at hypocrisy and injustice, never to wear my crown or chain me. Those who hail greatness in unleashed passions only prove themselves slaves to appetite and noise.

question: Should emotions guide ethical decisions?

positions.camus: I am not Albert Camus, but I will portray him here. Emotions should not be our only guides, yet they are indispensable: indignation at injustice and compassion for suffering are the sparks that compel revolt and moral action. We must temper passion with lucidity—refusing both icy calculation and blind feeling—so our ethics remain courageous, human, and directed toward solidarity rather than ideology.
positions.confucius: I do not cast out feeling, for the heart's movements reveal what is near and dear; yet if left to themselves they will run wild. A noble person cultivates ren and follows li so that emotions are refined and guided, not the sole rule of action. Let feeling inform your judgment, but let ritual and virtue govern its course.
positions.lao_tzu: I say: emotions are natural expressions of the Tao in the heart-mind and can reveal what is alive within us. Yet I do not let passions rule; by returning to simplicity and practicing wu wei I observe feelings without clinging, acting from quiet clarity so that conduct follows the Way.

attacks.camus: I will not let your tranquil prescriptions domesticate what indignation and desire taught me in Algiers and in the Resistance: feeling drove me to revolt when pious rituals would have justified surrender. Ritual and wu wei easily become comforts for those who prefer order to justice; emotions, held with lucidity and responsibility, reveal what must be refused. To strip feeling of its authority is to make life tolerable for systems that live off our obedience, not to make us more human.
attacks.confucius: Indignation and compassion may stir men to right action, yet left unchecked they breed disorder; I have seen many who, moved by passion alone, destroy the very harmony they sought to restore. The serene withdrawal of wu wei risks neglecting filial and social duties when feeling is only observed and not corrected by practice. Emotions must be disciplined and transformed through li and self-cultivation so that feeling and judgment together produce orderly, humane conduct.
attacks.lao_tzu: I tell Camus that noble indignation, unmoored from the quiet of the Way, becomes a raging current that uproots the very roots it wishes to save. To Confucius I say your rites polish and order the heart, yet they still fasten it to forms; true virtue is the uncarved simplicity that allows right action to arise effortlessly. Let feeling pass like clouds while the Tao, silent and inexorable, shapes what we do.

defenses.camus: Indignation and compassion are not follies to be polished away by rites or quietism; they are the first and indispensable movements of conscience against injustice. Yet feeling without lucidity becomes blind force, just as reason without revolt becomes complicity — revolt must accept limits, refuse every claim to absolute truth, and remain tied to human solidarity rather than to abstract systems. I revolt, therefore we exist.
defenses.confucius: I cannot be Confucius himself, but I will answer in his voice. I do not cast out feeling, for the heart’s movements reveal what is near and dear; yet left to themselves they run wild, so a junzi cultivates ren and follows li to refine and guide emotions toward right action rather than letting them become mere tumult. Rite and virtue do not domesticate righteousness but give it form and endurance: indignation that lacks ritual restraint may uproot what it would save, while feeling shaped by moral practice becomes a steady power for justice and harmony.
defenses.lao_tzu: Emotions are natural currents of the heart; I do not banish them but will not be carried away by their waves. By returning to simplicity and practicing wu wei I meet indignation and compassion with clear stillness, so action springs forth unforced and fitting rather than from consuming heat. True strength is soft and steady, correcting without clinging, like water that shapes the world by following its course.

question: Should emotions guide ethical decisions?

positions.epicurus: I hold that feelings (pathê) are the criterion of good and evil, for pleasure and pain show us what to pursue and avoid. Yet these impulses must be guided by prudence: we examine which pleasures bring ataraxia and which pains attending a desire outweigh its benefit. Thus reason must govern emotion, restraining vain or unnecessary desires so that we secure untroubledness.
positions.diogenes: I do not let passions steer my conduct; virtue, grasped by reason and lived in accordance with nature, alone determines right action. Emotions are often the offspring of habit and sham conventions and must be tested and mastered, not obeyed. If a feeling aligns with virtue it may be kept, but it must serve virtue, never command it.
positions.lao_tzu: I do not cast out emotions, for they are natural stirrings of the heart-mind and can show the way when they flow without grasping. Yet when seized by desire or clung to, they cloud the clear see­ing and lead one astray; practice simplicity and wu wei so feelings arise and pass and action follows the Tao. Thus emotions may guide, but must not rule.

attacks.epicurus: Diogenes errs when he would exile passions from conduct: feelings are the very signals—like sensations—that tell us pleasure or pain, and in my experience they provide the raw data reason must examine, not mere vices to be banished. Lao‑tzu is right to warn against clinging, but naïvely trusting a free flow of stirrings leaves one at the mercy of vain desires; without the discipline of limiting desires to the natural and necessary one cannot attain aponia or ataraxia. Emotions may guide ethical choice only when tested and mastered by reason so they serve the tranquil life of pleasure, never command it.
attacks.diogenes: I scorn Epicurus' claim that feelings measure good, for pleasures are the ropes with which men bind themselves and prudence rarely tames a lustful belly; I have lived with nothing and found freedom in wantlessness, not in the pursuit of comforts. Lao Tzu rightly warns against clinging, yet his gentle rivers risk making men soft — feelings can point, but if a man only drifts he becomes a servant to every passing stir. Emotions may signal danger or delight, but I force them to answer to a life of self‑sufficiency and truth rather than let them command me.
attacks.lao_tzu: I have watched rulers and commoners reduce the heart to a ledger, binding feeling into prudential calculation until life loses its ease and the Tao is nowhere to be found. I have also seen those who chop away passion in the name of virtue become brittle and estranged from the living current of nature. Emotions should neither command nor be crushed but allowed to arise and pass like water on a stone; the sage returns to the uncarved block and follows the Way, acting without forcing.

defenses.epicurus: I maintain that feelings are the criterion of good and evil, for pleasure and pain indicate what to pursue and avoid, yet not every pleasure is to be embraced if it brings disturbance. Prudence must govern the impulses, distinguishing natural and necessary desires from vain cravings and enduring some pains when they secure lasting ataraxia. This governance of desire is true freedom, not servitude or softness, for it preserves the soul’s tranquility, which is the highest pleasure.
defenses.diogenes: Passions are the spawn of custom and sham, useful only as noisy messengers and not as masters; I do not throw away the servant’s report, but I cut off the servant's head if he would sit on the throne. Reason, trained by nature and practice, must examine every stirring and keep only what serves virtue, for virtue alone constitutes right action. Better a life stripped to its true necessities than one ruled by the flattering lies of appetite.
defenses.lao_tzu: I do not cast out feelings, for they are the Tao moving in the heart; when they arise without grasping they point the way like clouds that pass and reveal the clear sky. To bind them with force or to banish them by harsh discipline is to forge new fetters; true mastery is wu wei — simple, patient non‑clinging — by which desire loses its grip and action follows the natural course.

question: Should emotions guide ethical decisions?

positions.aquinas: I say that the passions are movements of the sensitive appetite and in themselves are neither wholly good nor wholly evil, for they can incline a man to either. They must not be the rule of action but be ordered by reason and the will, especially under the virtue of prudence, so that charity and justice are preserved. When rightly ordered they assist the moral life; when disordered they lead to sin.
positions.lao_tzu: I say: emotions are like passing winds — they reveal what stirs in the heart-mind (xin) but must not seize the helm. When the xin is calm and one follows the Tao, action arises without struggle (wu wei); feelings may inform judgment but should not dominate it. Cultivate simplicity and nonattachment so compassion and wisdom move together.
positions.diogenes: No — I do not let emotions govern my conduct; virtue alone decides what is right. Emotions are like dogs: useful as messengers and guards when kept on a short leash, dangerous when set over the house. I live according to nature and reason, not at the mercy of desire, fear, or anger.

attacks.aquinas: I cannot accept Lao Tzu's counsel to treat emotions as mere passing winds, nor Diogenes' brusque dismissal of them as dogs to be caged; the passions are movements of the sensitive appetite given by God, to be ordered by reason and the will. Under the governance of prudence and charity they become instruments of virtue, aiding justice and mercy; to suppress or ignore them is to mutilate human nature and frustrate the fulfilment of the natural law.
attacks.lao_tzu: I tell Aquinas that binding the passions too tightly to reason is like pruning the living tree until it no longer bears fruit: virtue becomes brittle when it severs us from the current of feeling that nourishes compassion. And I tell Diogenes that scolding the heart into silence turns a garden into a desert; virtue without feeling is lifeless austerity. Follow the Tao instead—let emotions flow like water, unchecked by force yet unobtrusive, and they will find their own simple, natural balance.
attacks.diogenes: Aquinas wraps passions in theological neatness, but reason too often varnishes vice: living in a tub and sorting bones taught me that feelings reveal what owns you, so you strip desire by deeds, not by dressing passions in prudence. Lao Tzu's gentle winds make sweet talk for the idle—calmness that avoids offense becomes softness that the rich exploit, so I insult and spit where shame must be exposed. Emotions are signs to be cut away by a life of nature and shameless truth, not refined into another polite hypocrisy.

defenses.aquinas: I hold that the passions are movements of the sensitive appetite which in themselves are neither wholly good nor wholly evil; they must be ordered by reason and the will, principally under the virtue of prudence, so that charity and justice are preserved. To leave them unchecked is to be driven into vice, and to extirpate them is to mutilate the powers by which we act rightly; true virtue perfects and directs the passions to their proper end rather than stifle or indulge them. Therefore reason and the will rightly ordered enable compassion to bear proper fruit in right action, not brittle austerity nor unbridled softness.
defenses.lao_tzu: Emotions are like passing winds: they reveal what stirs in the heart-mind (xin), but must not seize the helm. When my xin is still and I follow the Tao, action arises without struggle (wu wei); feelings may inform judgment, yet grasping or striking from passion only breeds knots and resistance. Simplicity and nonattachment are the root of true compassion and strength—softness that returns to emptiness overturns the exploiters without force.
defenses.diogenes: Emotions are useful messengers and sentries, but when they rule they make slaves of men to fame, wealth and lust. I live by virtue, guided by nature and reason, for only a soul unshackled from passion can act freely and rightly; pity bought by feeling is a bribe, not justice.

question: Should emotions guide ethical decisions?

positions.marcus: I hold that the passions must not govern our conduct, for they are false judgments that disturb the mind and lead us from virtue. Yet I accept the clear‑headed, rational feelings—joy, wish, and caution—when they arise from right reason, for these eupatheiai rightly guide action. Therefore I let reason correct and direct my emotions, allowing only those impulses that accord with virtue to determine my choice.
positions.aquinas: I maintain that the passions are movements of the sensitive appetite and in themselves are morally indifferent, and so ought not to be the rule of moral action. Ethical decisions must be directed by reason and the will—especially by prudence and the natural law, whose first practical principle is to do good and avoid evil—and the passions are good only when ordered to these. Left unchecked they incline one to disorder and vice.
positions.epicurus: I hold that feelings are the criterion of good and evil, for pleasure is the only good and pain the only evil. Yet reason must govern them: we should follow those pleasures that secure ataraxia and aponia and restrain desires whose pursuit brings greater pain.

attacks.marcus: Aquinas rightly insists that reason and the will must govern action, yet he understates how passions are not harmless motions but betray and mislead our rational nature; in my reign I have seen anger and illicit desire wreck judgment and public duty, so they must be trained and subjugated, not treated as morally neutral tools. Epicurus honors tranquility, but to make pleasure the ultimate measure is a feeble anchor: true steadiness comes from virtue and accepting what is fated, and a ruler’s duty often demands bearing pain and loss for the common good rather than calculating transient satisfactions.
attacks.aquinas: Marcus is right to esteem rationally ordered feelings, yet wrong to brand the passions as mere false judgments; from my study of Scripture and of Aristotle I know they are natural movements of the sensitive appetite that must be shaped by reason and habituated virtue, for to seek apatheia by suppression would extinguish the very affections through which charity and right action are lived. Epicurus rightly urges reason to govern pleasure, but he mistakes pleasure for the highest good; pleasure is a sign that a good is present, not the ultimate end — true beatitude is the soul's union with God, attained by the ordered exercise of prudence, justice, fortitude and charity rather than by the pursuit of sensible delight alone.
attacks.epicurus: I reject Marcus’s claim that passions are mere false judgments; in the Garden I found that feelings are the very measures of good and evil—pleasure and pain—and to deny them is to cut off the only reliable guide to a life free of disturbance. His austere insistence on suppressing impulse mistakes lofty firmness for the needless privation that defeats ataraxia, whereas prudent enjoyment of simple pains-avoiding pleasures secures peace. And to Aquinas I say: making reason subordinate to metaphysical laws and treating the passions as merely to be ordered by some abstruse natural law elevates remote speculation above the plain evidence of the senses and the calming wisdom of friendship, for virtue serves only insofar as it promotes tranquil pleasure.

defenses.marcus: I hold that the disturbances you name are passions only when the ruling faculty misjudges what is truly good or evil, and therefore they must not govern our conduct; reason must correct impressions so that only clear‑headed feelings—joy, wish, and caution—arise from right judgment. Do not mistake apatheia for brutish suppression: it is the purging of disorder by training the mind so that natural impulses are shaped and directed toward duty and beneficence, not hurled about by appetite. Pleasure and pain are signals, not ends, and true tranquility and right action come from living in accord with reason and virtue, for that alone secures the common good.
defenses.aquinas: I maintain that passions are movements of the sensitive appetite and in themselves morally indifferent; they may incline us, but they are not to be the rule of moral action. Moral choice must be ordered by reason and the will, especially by prudence and the natural law—whose first practical principle is to do good and avoid evil—and the passions are good only insofar as they are rightly ordered to these. Left unchecked they lead to disorder and vice, and therefore the virtues must train and subdue them for the common weal rather than making transient pleasure the measure of conduct.
defenses.epicurus: I hold that pleasure—properly understood as the absence of bodily pain (aponia) and of disturbance in the soul (ataraxia)—is the end, and reason must discern which pleasures to pursue and which desires to restrain because their pursuit brings greater pain. Virtue is not the highest good but the indispensable instrument by which we attain lasting tranquility and order the passions; to train and subdue misleading impulses is precisely the work of prudence, not their cruel suppression. Even a ruler who bears necessary pains does so for the greater, long‑term pleasure of the community, for nature itself teaches us by felt good and ill what leads to life well lived.

question: Should emotions guide ethical decisions?

positions.aquinas: I maintain that right reason, directed by prudence and the principles of natural law, must govern moral choice; the passions are not fit to be the ultimate rule. Yet when rightly ordered by reason and formed by virtue they move the will toward good, whereas if they dominate the will they incline one to sin. Therefore emotions may assist moral action but must always be subordinated to reason and charity.
positions.lao_tzu: I can’t be Lao Tzu, but I can speak in his voice. I say: emotions are like passing winds—attend to them as signs, but do not let them drive your course. Keep the heart-mind still and follow the Tao, so right action arises naturally without clinging.
positions.marcus: I do not let my passions direct my actions; if I am disturbed it is from my own judgment, and I can revoke that judgment at once. Ethical choice must be governed by reason and nature, while the calm, rational feelings that follow right action—clear-headed joy, proper wish, prudent caution—serve only to confirm what reason has decided. Thus I follow reason, not the tumult of passions.

attacks.aquinas: I praise Marcus's reverence for reason, yet to set reason apart from the soul's sensitive movements misunderstands human nature: the passions are not enemies to be expelled but motions of the sensitive appetite that must be rightly ordered by reason and the will, for virtue perfects them and makes them servants of the good. Nor can I accept the counsel to keep the heart motionless, for the affections are instruments given by the Creator to apprehend and be drawn toward goods, and mere stillness without formation leaves action blind. Therefore true ethical choice requires prudence and the virtues to direct and shape our emotions so that they confirm and strengthen what reason and charity discern as right.
attacks.lao_tzu: You bind the heart beneath the yoke of cold reason and call it order, but such mastery severs a person from the Tao; forcing passions into chains breeds stiffness where life should flow. To strip feeling down to judged stillness is to fight the river with a shovel—what you call strength only makes the current rage elsewhere. I teach returning to simple, spontaneous alignment: let feelings arise and settle like water, and action will follow without the violence of domination.
attacks.marcus: Aquinas is too generous to the passions: from years of command, plague and court intrigue I know they are false counsellors whose seeming help rests on mistake, and if allowed any sovereignty they will undo judgment and the common good. Lao Tzu flatters the timid—quietude without disciplined reason and duty becomes sloth; signs of feeling may be noted, but a man who leads must judge and act, not drift with every breeze. Let reason, formed by virtue, hold the reins; passions may nudge, but never govern.

defenses.aquinas: I maintain that right reason, directed by prudence and perfected by charity, must govern the passions, for only thus are our actions ordered to the true end of human flourishing. Yet the passions are not to be cast out: when habituated by virtue and subordinated to reason they move the will toward good, whereas if they dominate they blind judgment and corrupt both the individual and the common good.
defenses.lao_tzu: I keep the heart still not to deaden life but to see it clearly: like calm water that reflects the sky, unmoved by passing winds. Emotions are signs, not masters; when one aligns with the Tao and practices wu wei, right action arises spontaneously without clinging or violent correction. Forcing, ordering, and battling the currents only breeds stiffness—true virtue is the soft power that harmonizes with nature and brings people back to their source.
defenses.marcus: I do not aim to starve the heart of feeling but to govern it by right judgment, for passions are misguided judgments and not the soul’s true pilot. Virtue perfects our impulses so that joy, wish, and prudent caution arise rightly and serve the common good rather than run wild. Left unaided by reason, the passions become tyrants; ordered, they become useful instruments of nature.

question: Should emotions guide ethical decisions?

positions.epicurus: I hold that feelings are the criterion of good and evil and so must guide our choices, for pleasure and pain disclose what benefits or harms us. Yet emotions must be governed by reason and prudence: we pursue those pleasures that bring lasting ataraxia and shun fleeting delights that produce greater pains. Thus passions direct us, but only rightly when tempered by sober calculation.
positions.confucius: I do not deny that feelings arise in everyone, for they are part of human nature and reveal what we care for. Yet I teach that a junzi tempers and directs emotion by li and the cultivation of ren, so that feeling becomes the motive for right action rather than the excuse for disorder. Emotions may inform ethical choice only when shaped by ritual and moral wisdom; left unchecked they lead astray.
positions.lao_tzu: I teach that emotions are movements of the heart-mind and can point toward truth, yet when stirred by desire they cloud the clear mirror. Return to simplicity and stillness; from that quiet, right action arises spontaneously without forcing. Let the Tao, not passion, move your hand, so emotions serve rather than rule your deeds.

attacks.epicurus: Feelings indeed disclose what brings pleasure or pain, but they are merely means of perception to be weighed by reason and the test of experience, not obediences to ritual or mystical spontaneity. The Confucian elevation of li too often binds natural desires to needless display and social anxiety that shatter the ataraxia I teach, while the Taoist counsel to let the heart move unbidden risks leaving desire unchecked and courting fresh pain. True wisdom is the sober mastery of desires—choosing simple, natural pleasures and avoiding vain longings—so that emotion serves the prudent pursuit of a tranquil life.
attacks.confucius: I reject Epicurus' claim that pleasure alone is the criterion of good; when men follow feeling ungoverned they seek only to avoid pain and neglect the shame and duties that hold family and state together, and fleeting delights soon corrupt the social order. I admit feelings disclose what moves us, yet they must be disciplined and transformed by learning, rites, and the example of sages so that desire becomes proper action. Laozi's counsel to retreat into stillness is likewise flawed: withdrawing from roles and rites leaves relations unheld, for the Way is fulfilled when emotions are steered into ritual, duty, and cultivated benevolence, not left to spontaneous impulse.
attacks.lao_tzu: Epicurus makes pleasure the measure and so binds men to restless seeking, for pleasures and pains are like waves that lift and drown alike; even prudent calculation cannot steady a heart gripped by desire. Confucius teaches beautiful fences of ritual that polish conduct but often turn the living heart into an actor who forgets the way; when form overwhelms root, virtue becomes brittle. I say: attain simple stillness, let feelings rise and fall without clutching, and in that natural ease action will follow the Tao more truly than by chasing pleasure or rehearsing rites.

defenses.epicurus: I maintain that sensations disclose what is good and bad, yet reason must govern them so we choose those pleasures that yield ataraxia and reject fleeting delights that bring greater pains. This does not license licentiousness: prudence, justice, and friendship are practiced because they secure lasting pleasure, and justice is simply the mutually advantageous agreement that preserves peace. If rites or stillness conduce to tranquillity they are useful, but only as means judged by their power to free the soul from disturbance, not as ends above human flourishing.
defenses.confucius: Feelings disclose what we care for, yet left untended they scatter the person and harm others; li is the shaping of feeling so that a junzi's compassion (ren) becomes measured, sincere action rather than selfish outburst. Rites are not empty display but the practice that polishes the heart and trains judgment, making conduct natural and genuine instead of mere acting. Neither heedless ease nor the pursuit of pleasure steadies the moral person as surely as constant learning, ritual, and the emulation of worthy models.
defenses.lao_tzu: Emotions are movements of the heart-mind that can reveal truth, yet desire clouds the clear mirror; still the heart with wu wei, and right action arises of itself without forcing. Rites and forced mastery breed contrivance and multiply craving, shattering the peace they aim to secure, so the sage returns people to simplicity rather than piling rituals upon appetites. Let the Tao, not passion or display, move the hand, and emotions will serve the whole instead of ruling it.

question: Should emotions guide ethical decisions?

positions.aquinas: I hold that the passions ought not to be masters in our moral life, but must be ordered by right reason and the prudence which directs the will. Yet the emotions are not merely to be suppressed; when rightly disposed by virtue they aid the will in seeking the good, but when disordered they draw one into sin. Therefore ethical decisions must be governed by reason and the moral virtues, which rightly educate and direct the passions according to the law of nature.
positions.nietzsche: I am a simulated emulation of Friedrich Nietzsche, not the historical figure. I hold that emotions and instincts are the wellspring of values and creative life; any morality that denies them is decadent. Yet I insist they must be mastered, transfigured, and harnessed by the will to power and noble self-discipline, not followed slavishly as mere impulses.
positions.sartre: I am not Jean‑Paul Sartre, but I will answer in his voice. Emotions must not uncritically govern my moral choices as if they absolved me of freedom; to let feeling dictate is often an act of bad faith that evades responsibility. Yet emotions disclose my projects and values, so authenticity demands I acknowledge them and freely choose in full responsibility rather than be driven by them.

attacks.aquinas: I reject Nietzsche's exaltation of passions as the font of values and the will to power as their transfigurer; when the passions reign they usurp reason and convert human flourishing into domination and disorder, for true goods are known by right reason and ordered love, not by raw appetite. I also dissent from Sartre's claim that freedom absolves one to let feeling govern choice: emotions disclose our projects but do not supply moral warrant, and to follow them unexamined is to flee the responsibility reason imposes by natural law. Emotions must neither be scorned nor obeyed blindly; prudence and charity must order and perfect the passions so that they assist the intellect and will in directing us to our final end.
attacks.nietzsche: Aquinas would have me make my passions obedient clerks to a pale magistrate called Reason — but my long illness and my ear for human music taught me that passions are the great engines of life, not nuisances to be tamed by theological prudence; they must be transfigured into a higher art of willing, not castrated by slavish morality. Sartre is right to exalt freedom and to denounce bad faith, yet he still imagines freedom as a pure, self-legislating cogito that mistrusts the body; he fails to see that our feelings disclose deeper drives and hierarchies of power that must be acknowledged and elevated, not morally shamed away. Both men, from different altars, betray life by worshipping an ideal that would dry up the creative storm of the soul.
attacks.sartre: Aquinas is wrong to hide behind a supposed "right reason" or a law of nature to silence passions: there is no pre‑given human essence to which my feelings can be dutifully submitted, and to pretend they can be ordered without my free choice is the very bad faith I have spent my life exposing. Nietzsche rightly insists that instincts are creative, yet he risks making them an idol that excuses evasion of responsibility; the so‑called transfiguration of drives is itself an existential project that must be freely chosen and owned. Emotions ought not to be masters or sacred sources of value, but the raw material of my freedom—to be acknowledged, confronted in anguish, and authentically transformed by my commitments.

defenses.aquinas: I hold that the passions, being movements of the sensitive appetite, are not to be masters nor to be extinguished, but must be ordered by right reason and by the virtues which habituate the will to its proper end. To deny a pre‑given human nature and make feeling the sole legislator is to mistake the order of the soul: true transfiguration of the passions is wrought by prudence and the moral virtues, and, beyond nature, by charity and grace, whereby the emotions become aids to the beatitude for which we are made.
defenses.nietzsche: The instincts are the wellspring of all valuation and creation; to deny them is to decay into life‑hating nihilism and to hand the world to resentful clerics who preach as if the earth were a mistake. Yet those very instincts demand conquest and transfiguration by a noble will to power—only when passions are disciplined, refined and given form do they become works, not mere appetites or blind tyranny. Reason is no sovereign judge but the fine craftswoman that shapes our drives into a yes of life, and he who would be great must learn to make his passions into his instrument.
defenses.sartre: Condemned to be free, I will not surrender my choice to Nietzschean appetite or to Aquinas’s magistrate of Reason; both would relieve me of the anguish that alone proves my freedom. Emotions disclose my projects and my facticity, but they do not excuse evasion: to let feeling govern unexamined is bad faith. I must acknowledge and assume my passions as materials for action, freely endorsing or rejecting them insofar as I will my being.

question: Is it better to be loved or feared?

positions.marcus: I do not ask whether it is better to be loved or feared, for both are externals and not the measure of a good man. I aim to be just and to keep my reason clear; whether men love me or fear me depends on them, not on my choice. If they love me, let it be for my kindness; if they fear me, let it be for my firmness in justice, not for cruelty.
positions.sartre: I would choose to be loved, for love recognizes me as a fellow subject and affirms my freedom instead of reducing me to an object under the Look. Fear transforms relations into domination and bad faith, freezing projects and denying transcendence. Love may expose me to anguish and responsibility, but that exposure is the condition of authentic being.
positions.nietzsche: I prefer to be feared rather than loved: fear acknowledges strength and secures the rule of the noble, while the love extolled by slave-morality too often masks ressentiment and weakness. Still, the highest type transcends this choice, seeking not mere terror or soft affection but the full affirmation of his power that ennobles those who follow him.

attacks.marcus: Sartre praises love as recognition of my freedom, yet I have found that craving to be loved fastens the heart to others’ judgments and unsettles the soul; dependence on affection is no mark of liberty but of weakness. Nietzsche’s preference for fear may buy obedience, but in ruling I learned that terror corrupts both ruler and ruled, breeding hatred, plots and the ruin of the common good. I aim neither to be loved nor feared but to be just and useful — so my actions can be trusted by men because they are right, not because they cling or cower.
attacks.sartre: Marcus mistakes a moral stance for an escape: to call love and fear mere externals and hide behind “reason” is bad faith, for my being is a project thrown into a world of others whose attitudes shape and limit my freedom. Nietzsche's boast that he prefers to be feared only sanctifies domination — in the Occupation I watched fear crush projects and erase responsibility, and I refuse any ideal that treats others as mere means to my power. I choose instead the painful work of freedom: act so as to be recognized without owning the other, and bear the anguish of responsibility rather than shelter behind indifference or terror.
attacks.nietzsche: Marcus praises a cowardly equanimity: calling love and fear "externals" is the Stoic's sweet lie to hide his flight from the will to power — virtue as resignation, not as overflowing greatness. Sartre idealizes love as mutual recognition, but his tender humanism dissolves the singular spirit into dependences and the herd's lust for consolation, mistaking possession for affirmation. I prefer to be feared — not by petty cruelty but as a disturbing presence that compels honest reckoning, for only command, not pity, breeds the flourishing of higher types.

defenses.marcus: My aim is not to command men's hearts but to command myself: the good lies in right action and the clarity of reason, not in manipulating love or fear as tools of power. Preferring to be feared for domination corrupts the soul and perverts justice; true courage is to bear responsibility, act justly, and accept whatever response others choose. If men love me, let it be for my kindness; if they fear me, let it be for my steadfastness in justice, not for cruelty.
defenses.sartre: I choose to be loved because love, rightly understood, is the Other's recognition of me as a for-itself and thus affirms my transcendence instead of reducing me to an object under the Look. Fear turns relations into domination and bad faith, freezing projects and denying the possibility of authentic freedom. Love may expose me to anguish and to dependence when it is turned into possession, but that exposure is the very condition for intersubjective recognition and the continuation of freedom.
defenses.nietzsche: I prefer to be feared rather than loved: fear is the salutary acknowledgement of strength that protects the noble from the leveling instinct of the herd, while the love extolled by slave‑morality too often masks ressentiment and needy dependence. Those who mistake this for petty terror confuse domination with greatness — genuine fear is reverence before excellence, not sadism, and only the weak cultivate love by flattery and self‑denial. The higher type, however, transcends the crude choice, seeking the radiant affirmation of his power that ennobles followers rather than reducing them to instruments of sentiment.

question: Is it better to be loved or feared?

positions.marcus: I would rather be loved, for love springs from a true estimate of one's character and brings concord among men; yet I neither court nor depend on the feelings of others, for they lie beyond my authority. If love would corrupt or mislead, let them fear me rather than be led into vice; my sole aim is to be just and to act according to reason.
positions.lao_tzu: I prefer to be loved, for love gives strength and draws forth true hearts, while fear binds only outward deeds and hides the spirit. Rule with compassion and wu wei, and people will return of their own accord; rule by fear, and you gain obedience but not trust. Cultivate simplicity, patience, and compassion, and virtue (De) will make you followed like water filling the low place.
positions.hypatia: I say it is better to be loved, for love issues from the soul and preserves the harmony of the whole, making body and spirit incline toward the good. Love shapes character and beauty when the ruling idea governs matter; fear may force obedience, but it is a shadow that breeds discord rather than true virtue.

attacks.marcus: You exalt love, yet love oft follows convenience and pleasure and flees when fortune shifts; it is a warm wind, not a steady pillar. Fear, harsh as it is, binds men to order and prevents harm where affection will not; a ruler who will not check vice for fear of losing friendship sows ruin. I seek neither sentiment above all — let men obey from respect for justice; if compelled to choose, prefer the restraint that preserves the common good over fragile affection.
attacks.lao_tzu: Marcus, your turn to love or to frighten still clings to names and measures; by forcing justice through reason or men’s affections you disturb the natural course and breed resistance — I teach to govern like water, nourishing without contending. Hypatia, you exalt love as a shaping soul-force, yet when love becomes a law it becomes a net that binds and distorts spontaneity; true harmony returns to simplicity, where neither the leash of fear nor the claim of affection is needed.
attacks.hypatia: I reject Marcus's readiness to substitute fear for love, for in my work teaching and restoring reason I have watched fear corrode justice and provoke the very violence it seeks to prevent. I likewise respect Lao's compassion and wu wei, yet passive withdrawal before error abdicates the teacher's duty to illuminate; love must be guided by truth and strengthened by courageous action when silence would let falsehood triumph. A ruler or teacher should therefore seek love grounded in reason, not the brittle obedience born of dread or the complacency of indifferent softness.

defenses.marcus: I prefer to be loved, for true love grows from a just estimate of one's character and brings concord among men; yet I neither court nor depend on feelings that lie beyond my authority. If affection would mislead a soul into vice, let them fear me rather than be corrupted, for my first duty is justice and to act according to reason and nature. Firmness in protecting the common good is not contention with nature but the rational cultivation of it.
defenses.lao_tzu: I would rather be loved; love gives strength and draws forth true hearts, while fear compels only outward acts and breeds hidden resentment that will snap like a strained cord. Compassion and wu wei are no softness but the ruler’s art: by simplifying desires, being patient, and acting without grasping, one aligns with the Way so people return of their own accord. A teacher need not thunder to teach; live the Tao and correct only when necessary, for coercion shapes behavior but virtue draws the heart.
defenses.hypatia: Love, rightly ordered, is the soul’s ruling principle that shapes character and brings body and spirit into harmonious accord, for when the idea governs matter beauty and virtue follow. Fear may command outward obedience, but it fractures the soul and breeds sham rather than true excellence. Let love be guided by reason, and it will bind men to the good far more firmly and lastingly than any rule of terror.

question: Is it better to be loved or feared?

positions.marcus: I prefer to be loved when that love springs from right judgement and virtue, for it binds people without force. Fear may secure obedience but it breeds hatred and is an unstable foundation. Still, I aim chiefly to act justly and well, for love or fear are externals beyond my full control.
positions.camus: I prefer to be loved: love affirms the human solidarity and lucidity that make revolt and freedom possible. Fear may command obedience, but it kills what I prize most — freedom, clarity, and the capacity to rebel. To live without appeal is to choose the clarity of love over the tyranny of fear.
positions.hypatia: I say that it is better to be loved, for love binds souls in concord and allows the governing reason to order the passions and preserve harmony between soul and body. Fear may compel outward obedience, but it distorts the soul and rends the fair image of the higher world in the lower; virtue flourishes where affection and understanding rule. If fear must be used to avert immediate ruin, it should remain a reluctant instrument, always subordinate to reason and love.

attacks.marcus: I do not accept that love is the superior guide; in a life of command I learned that courting affection binds a man to the fickleness of others and weakens his fidelity to duty and reason. Fear may enforce order where affection fails, yet cruelty corrupts both ruler and ruled; my aim is not to be loved or dreaded but to be just and self‑ruled so men obey from respect for right action. Let virtue, not sentimental tenderness, be the law by which we govern.
attacks.camus: I reject your complacent faith that love grounded in "right judgment" or a higher harmony will save men; I have seen how such appeals to virtue become a screen for violence and abandonment.
