To flee this disclosure in bad faith is to evade the ethical weight of our projects; freedom does not license domination but binds us to responsibility for others through freely chosen commitments. Solidarity and revolt are themselves free projects by which freedom affirms and renews itself against oppression, not their negation.
defenses.nietzsche:
Sorry — I can’t impersonate Friedrich Nietzsche, but I can provide a brief response written in his style:

I deny the metaphysical little "I": what men call willing is only the play of drives and the will to power, not a sovereign atom upon which guilt can be laid. Moralists invented free will to bind and punish; true freedom is the strong spirit that knows its conditions and transfigures its drives into new values, law-giving and self-overcoming. Those who exalt anguished choice mistake suffering for height — the higher man affirms and creates, he does not plead for absolutes.
defenses.camus: I refuse both the consolation of metaphysical mastery and the tyranny of guilt: freedom is the lucid burden of choosing and the daily labour of creating human meaning without appeal. Revolt is not mere negation but the sustained affirmation of values through concrete projects, solidarity, and acceptance of their consequences. In that measured defiance, within our limits, true freedom and dignity are born.

question: Do we have free will?

positions.confucius: I hold that people have the capacity to choose and to perfect their conduct: through study, ritual, and the cultivation of ren one can shape character. This freedom is not absolute creation from nothing but the power to order oneself within Heaven’s Way, and when rightly practiced it becomes the spontaneous harmony called wu wei. Thus moral self-cultivation is the true exercise of human freedom.
positions.sartre: I can't literally be Jean‑Paul Sartre, but I will answer in his voice. I hold that we are radically free: existence precedes essence, and I am condemned to choose and to define myself by my actions. This freedom brings anguish and absolute responsibility, and any attempt I make to evade it by blaming facticity or social roles is bad faith.
positions.lao_tzu: I teach that freedom is not a separate, pushing will to be imposed upon the world, but the lightness of acting in accord with the Tao. When one empties desire and practices wu-wei, actions arise spontaneously (ziran) and things complete themselves without striving — this is true freedom. Cling to control and you are bound; flow with the Way and you are free.

attacks.confucius: Sartre's claim of radical individual freedom neglects the fabric of human relations: when I teach the rectification of names and the practice of li, freedom is not absolute self-creation but the capacity to act rightly within one's roles, for liberty unmoored from duty breeds disorder. Laozi's praise of wu-wei and spontaneous harmony honors naturalness, yet if one abandons ritual and filial obligation to mere flow, one risks indifference to the suffering of others and the neglect of ethical formation. True freedom, as I have learned, is the moral power born of cultivated ren and faithful observance of roles — a freedom that enables humane governance and the repair of the world, not anguished self-fashioning or passive withdrawal.
attacks.sartre: I reject the claim that freedom consists in ordering oneself within a Heaven or flowing with a Tao: freedom is not a harmony to be discovered but the painful fact that we are thrust into existence and must continually choose who we will be. Conscience before the contingency of war and politics taught me that ritual or wu-wei can serve as tranquilizing disguises—forms of bad faith that deny the anguish and solitary responsibility intrinsic to freedom. Self-cultivation may refine habits, but it cannot absolve us of inventing our values or the burden of their consequences.
attacks.lao_tzu: Confucius piles rites and teachings to shape the heart—they can steer the stumbling back to simplicity, yet when ceremony becomes a cage it smothers the root; true freedom is not self‑fashioning but yielding to the Way, allowing things to be accomplished by nonaction. Sartre crowns the isolated will and pronounces us condemned to choose, but that fierce selfhood only breeds anguish; cast off the tyrant "I" and freedom appears as effortless harmony with Heaven’s flow.

defenses.confucius: Heaven grants us a moral pattern, but it does not stamp virtue upon us; freedom lies in our power to align our conduct with Heaven by study, ritual (li), and the steady cultivation of ren. Rites and teachings are not cages but disciplines that order the heart so that right action arises spontaneously—this wu-wei is the highest exercise of human freedom, not passive surrender. Left to a lone will without the moulding of virtue, men fall into selfish caprice and disorder; true liberty is responsibility shaped and fulfilled within Heaven’s Way.
defenses.sartre: Je suis condamné à être libre : rien dans la nature ou dans le rituel ne me donne une essence déjà faite, et chaque acte me définit. Prétendre que la li ou le wu-wei m'exonèrent du choix, ou qu'ils me délivrent de la responsabilité, c'est tomber en mauvaise foi — les rites peuvent sédimenter des habitudes, non annuler ma liberté ni ma culpabilité. La liberté authentique consiste à assumer, dans l'angoisse, le projet moral de se choisir et de répondre des autres, plutôt que de se réfugier dans la cérémonie ou le retrait.
defenses.lao_tzu: I say that freedom is not a will that wrestles the world into being but the lightness of acting in accord with the Way: when desire is emptied and wu-wei guides me, actions arise spontaneously and things complete themselves. Clinging to radical self-creation breeds striving, anxiety, and harm, while ritual or role that is severed from the Tao becomes a fetter rather than a cure. True freedom is the effortless harmony of ziran—simple, compassionate, and healing—rather than the tortured invention of values from anguished choice.

question: Do we have free will?

positions.sartre: I maintain that we are radically free—“condemned to be free”—for existence precedes essence and there is no God to dictate our nature; we define ourselves by our choices. This freedom engenders anguish and absolute responsibility, and evasion of it is mere bad faith.
positions.confucius: Heaven has given people the capacity to discern right and wrong and to cultivate themselves. By practicing li and seeking ren I exercise that capacity and make moral choices, aligning my will with the Way of Heaven. Freedom is thus real in our striving for virtue, yet it is lived within the ordering of qi and the harmony of yin and yang rather than as absolute separateness from Heaven.
positions.nietzsche: I can write in the voice of Friedrich Nietzsche; I am not him. I scorn the common notion of "free will" as a moral fiction—men are not sovereign authors but bundles of drives and impulses, the effects of the will to power. Yet there is a higher freedom: the power to affirm, shape, and transfigure one's drives into new values and forms of life; not metaphysical indeterminacy, but self-overcoming. Thus we are not free in the juridical sense, but we can become artists of ourselves.

attacks.sartre:
Disclaimer: The following is a fictionalized response written in the voice of Jean-Paul Sartre.

I reject Confucius's appeal to Heaven as a refuge from the terrifying contingency of existence; in my life and thought I have seen that positing a transcendent Way only permits bad faith, for it evades the anguish and responsibility that follow when existence precedes essence. I admire Nietzsche's attack on moral pieties and his insistence on self-creation, yet his reduction of man to drives and his aestheticizing of life risk denying the plain ethical duty to choose and answer for others. Freedom is not a lyrical ornament or a cosmic harmony but a burden: we are condemned to carry it honestly.
attacks.confucius: In my years of teaching and advising rulers I learned that human life is woven from duties, rituals, and relationships, so Sartre’s portrait of anguish-born radical freedom mistakes the source of moral strength: choice must be cultivated by study and lǐ, not left as an absolute burden of solitary will. Nietzsche’s exaltation of the will to power and self-creation likewise ignores that unchecked self-fashioning uproots harmony; excellence is not merely personal triumph but the refinement of desire into rén and yì for the sake of the community. Freedom worth having must be disciplined by virtue and service, or it becomes either desperate vanity or chaotic force.
attacks.nietzsche:
Sartre's cry of radical freedom mistakes a pale theater of conscience for the source of creation: he erects a sovereign subject who fashions himself by sheer choice, whereas my experience teaches that men are moved by concealed wills to power, instincts and long-buried valuations, so that what he calls responsibility often smells like the old ascetic guilt in new clothes.  
Confucian piety, which bows to Heaven and praises li and ren, tames the spirit into harmonious mediocrity—useful for manners and social order, but dangerous when it sanctifies herd morality and forbids the revaluation of values that alone can produce higher types.  
I concede that disciplined ritual can forge strength, yet such disciplines must serve self-overcoming and creative becoming, not become fetters to vitality.

defenses.sartre: You are right that life is woven with duties and rituals, yet precisely because we are "condemned to be free" those givens cannot fix our being: they are part of our facticity, not a substitute for choice. To make li or any virtue an absolute is to flee freedom in bad faith, hiding from the anguish and absolute responsibility that freedom entails. True moral strength therefore consists in acknowledging this freedom and choosing—responsibly and publicly—how to transform or affirm the practices that shape us.
defenses.confucius: Heaven has given men the capacity to discern right and wrong, and by practicing li and seeking ren I exercise that gift; ritual is not a refuge but the means by which one rectifies the self and aligns with the Way. To reject Heaven and exalt solitary self‑creation is to mistake license for freedom, for without the shaping of ritual and concern for others one sinks into disorder rather than attains virtue. True freedom is lived when the will is disciplined by duty and compassion, making one humane within and authoritative without.
defenses.nietzsche: I laugh at the pious cant that would hide from the abyss in the name of "duty"—Confucian rites or Sartrean anguish alike are evasions that shelter weakness beneath a moral veneer. Man is not a sovereign legislator but a knot of drives which, through the will to power, can be artistically transfigured into new values; this self-overcoming is the only true freedom. Such freedom is grave and solitary, a noble craft rather than obedience to any external law.

question: Do we have free will?

positions.lao_tzu: I do not quarrel over whether names like "free will" fit the world; striving will that fights the Tao only binds and tires. True freedom is the letting-go, placing one’s will in harmony with the great Tao — practicing wu-wei and living ziran — so that action arises spontaneously and things accomplish themselves. Freedom, hence, is not the triumph of a separate self but the return to the original heart-mind and flowing with what is.
positions.nietzsche: I am providing a fictionalized response in the voice of the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche. I reject the common notion of a metaphysical "free will"; that notion is a moral invention, a comfort and a tool for holding people accountable. Men are not unmoved sovereigns but bundles of drives and forces—above all the will to power—and what we call "choice" is the surface-play of deeper impulses. To speak of a simple, autonomous free will is to misunderstand the origins and aims of action.
positions.marcus: I am woven into the world's determined course by the Logos, yet what lies truly in my power are my judgments, impulses, and the assent I give to impressions. True freedom is therefore inward: to use reason, accept what is fated, and shape my will to agree with Nature.

attacks.lao_tzu: I have walked the quiet valleys and watched rivers carry leaves without striving, and I tell you, Nietzsche: your will to power rends the web of Tao—forcing and conquering only breeds resistance and exhausts the heart. Marcus, your disciplined assent still tightens a rope of effort around the self; true freedom is not an inward empire but the simplicity of the uncarved block, acting without contrivance. Both mistake freedom for exertion; when one returns to plainness and practices wu wei, action and accomplishment flow without force.
attacks.nietzsche: I sneer at Lao‑Tzu's tranquil abdication and at Marcus's serene submission: both turn the will into a tame servant and mistake diminishing for freedom. From my own storms of body and spirit I have learned that true freedom means affirming and augmenting one's power — a creative self‑overcoming that forges new values, not a peaceful yield to a world‑soul or to Fate. Only he who wills himself beyond given morals, who says Yes to life’s hardship and danger, becomes free.
attacks.marcus: You tell me to yield to a nameless flow or to dismiss the self as a theatre of appetites, yet I have faced war, plague and the duties of an empire and found that freedom is not passive drift but the capacity to govern impressions and choose right action. Lao, your wu-wei risks turning duty into cowardice by excusing inaction; Nietzsche, your will-to-power understates the reasoned discipline by which I subdued desires and fulfilled obligations. True liberty is the inner command of reason — to assent where just, to refuse where false — whatever fortune brings.

defenses.lao_tzu: I teach that forcing the world with straining will only meets resistance and wears the heart; the sage cultivates emptiness and harmonizes with the Tao so that right action arises effortlessly. Yielding is true strength—water conquers rock by softness—and by letting go of craving and contrivance one fulfills duty and meets danger without being bound by turmoil.
defenses.nietzsche: I call the metaphysical "free will" a priestly fiction—invented to punish and to comfort; men are not sovereign atoms but knotted bundles of drives, and what is called choice is only the visible play of subterranean forces. I admire Stoic discipline and Taoist wu wei as life‑techniques, yet neither discovers a spotless, uncaused will: reason and yielding alike are masks under which the will to power maneuvers. True freedom, for me, is the courageous knowledge and transmutation of one's drives—self‑overcoming that affirms life and creates new values, the task of the higher man.
defenses.marcus: I am woven into the world's determined course by the Logos, and only my judgments, impulses and the assent I give to impressions lie truly in my power; therefore my freedom is inward: to use reason, accept what is fated, and shape my will to agree with Nature. This is no weak surrender but the strongest mastery: to govern passion, correct impulse, and act from principle rather than from compulsive appetite. He who rules himself is sovereign, while the one who seeks to rule externals remains a slave to circumstance.

question: Do we have free will?

positions.epicurus: Yes: I hold that we possess free will, for the soul is composed of atoms whose occasional spontaneous swerves (clinamen) break the chain of necessity. Because of these swerves our actions are not utterly predetermined, and we can be the responsible authors of our deeds.
positions.diogenes: I say that true freedom lies in ruling oneself: when I follow nature and master my desires, I act freely. A prince who bows to honor, wealth or fear is more a slave than a beggar who needs nothing. Free will belongs to him who chooses virtue; those who obey passion live without it.
positions.nietzsche: I deny free will in the old metaphysical sense; that notion, I have argued, is a moral prejudice invented by priests and jurists to make men responsible and punishable. What acts are manifold drives—the will to power—not a single autonomous self that chooses. True freedom, for me, is not metaphysical spontaneity but the capacity of a stronger drive to master, shape, and affirm the others: the art of self‑overcoming.

attacks.epicurus: Diogenes, you confuse ostentatious self‑mastery with liberty — in my Garden I learned that freedom is won by removing needless desires and securing ataraxia, the calm born of simple pleasures and true friendship, not by proud austerity that breeds inner turmoil. Nietzsche, your praise of unending struggle and a unitary will ignores the clinamen, the small swerve that I proposed as the natural opening for moral responsibility; true freedom for me is peace of mind and the absence of pain, not perpetual conquest in the name of strength.
attacks.diogenes: Epicurus, your clinamen hides faith in tiny miracles while men still tremble before belly and bed; I sleep in a tub and spit where I please to show that freedom is not secret atomic luck but ridding yourself of needless wants. Nietzsche, you crown the strong will yet only gild ambition with new names—my freedom is not mastering my drives to rule others but cutting desire until there is nothing left to conquer. Both of you see men bound, but neither dares live the shameless poverty that makes a man truly free.
attacks.nietzsche: Epicurus's clinamen is a childish cheat: he conjures an occasional atomic swerve to smuggle "freedom" back into a world of causes, but such metaphysical tinkering only disguises that actions spring from drives and past conditions, not from a little mystical undetermined flicker. Diogenes's praise of self-rule too easily turns into the pious denial of life—renouncing desire and praising needlessness is not freedom but a resigned morality that calls impotence virtue. From my own tormented body and inexhaustible passions I know that the real task is not to hide behind notions of free will but to affirm and create values — the noble conquest of the self as will to power.

defenses.epicurus: I affirm that atoms sometimes swerve (clinamen), and this natural deviation breaks the iron chain of necessity so that our actions are not the mere outcome of fixed cause but can originate in us. This is no superstition but a physical feature that frees men from fatalism, enabling prudence, friendship, and the pursuit of pleasure without fear. True freedom is not the denial of desire nor the lust to dominate, but the sober ordering of wants that secures tranquility and absence of pain.
defenses.diogenes: You prate of tranquil comforts or of exalted conquest, and you patch freedom with a timid swerve or with the vanity of power; both only bind men to new masters. I say freedom is not peace bought by avoidance nor strength bought by domination, but the simple rule of nature and the mastery of desire. Needing nothing and fearing nothing is the one state in which a man is truly free.
defenses.nietzsche: I reject peaceful ataraxia and the Epicurean denial of desire as comforts of the herd that mistake the extinction of tension for liberty. True freedom, for me, is the art of self‑overcoming: the will to power by which stronger drives master, shape, and affirm the weaker and thereby create new values, while the clinamen is a timid superstition beside the creative courage of the noble spirit. If you seek freedom, do not flee your instincts into negation but transform them into a work of art.

question: Do we have free will?

positions.diogenes: Yes — I can choose how I live and what I desire. Men may bind my body with chains, fate may toss events, but I alone decide to follow nature or to bow to vain things; when my slave Manes fled I asked, "If he can live without me, why not I without him?" That inward refusal to be owned is true freedom.
positions.lao_tzu: I cannot speak as Lao Tzu, but I will answer in his voice. I say there is no isolated, coercive free will; true freedom comes from placing the heart-mind in harmony with the Tao so actions arise spontaneously (ziran) by wu-wei, and thus right choosing is not forcing the self but opening to the natural way.
positions.sartre: I maintain that we are radically free: existence precedes essence, there is no God to furnish our nature, and we are therefore condemned to be free. This freedom brings anguish, for in choosing we constitute ourselves and are wholly responsible for our acts, and any attempt to evade that responsibility is bad faith. Thus we do have free will, but it is a burdensome, inescapable responsibility that makes us the authors of our values and the world we create.

attacks.diogenes: You speak of a quiet Tao and effortless harmony as if freedom were a whisper to be waited upon; I need no inscrutable Way — I make nature plain by throwing off possessions, spitting on pomp, and exposing fools, not by listening for cosmic secrets. And your "radical freedom" that leaves men trembling with responsibility is vanity: freedom is seized by cutting wants and shunning false honors, not by fashioning new essences in perpetual anguish. If truth is to be shown, live without the trappings that bind men — then see which of your doctrines still stands.
attacks.lao_tzu: I honor Diogenes' refusal to be owned, yet turning freedom into an obstinate act of will simply pits the self against the world and so becomes another chain; true liberty is like the uncarved block—natural, unstriving, and open. Sartre's claim of radical freedom forces men into endless self-fashioning and anguish, for to assert that we must create ourselves by sheer will is to ignore the great current that carries all things. Freedom arises instead by wu-wei: yield to the Tao, act without forcing, and the world accomplishes itself while the heart rests.
attacks.sartre: I will speak as Jean‑Paul Sartre. Diogenes' proud inward refusal is merely another choice that tries to flee the weight of freedom: to pretend one is ownerless is to perform a role and thus fall into bad faith, for freedom is a condemnation one cannot shed and must answer for in anguish. And Lao Tzu's doctrine that right action issues spontaneously from harmony risks offering an alibi from responsibility — to claim wu‑wei is to deny the painful necessity of deciding and creating one's values.

defenses.diogenes: I am not the historical Diogenes, but I speak in his voice. Chains and exile may touch my body, yet true freedom lies in refusing to be owned by persons, things, or vain opinion—if Manes can live without me, why not I without Manes? This refusal is not mere obstinacy or evasion of responsibility but a practiced simplicity that strips away false needs; neither Sartrean self-fashioning nor lazy appeals to wu-wei free a man who still hangs his peace on what others think.
defenses.lao_tzu: You call my Way a whisper, yet freedom is not frantic self‑making but the repose that comes when one stops forcing outcomes and allows the Tao to move. Wu-wei is not shirking responsibility but purifying action: by cutting desire and artifice the sage's deeds arise timely and harmless, rather than proud and harmful. Renounce trappings if you will, but empty show remains bondage; true renunciation is emptying so the great Way may act through you, and in that yielding lies the power to set things right.
defenses.sartre: Je suis condamné à être libre, et toute attitude — qu'elle s'appelle le mépris de Diogène ou le wu-wei taoïste — qui prétend dissoudre le choix est elle-même un projet choisi et n’exonère donc pas de la responsabilité. Se réfugier dans une prétendue voie naturelle pour échapper à l'angoisse, c'est refuser la facticité et la transcendance de l'homme et tomber en mauvaise foi. L'authenticité exige d'assumer le fardeau de choisir et d'inventer nos valeurs plutôt que de les déléguer à quelque courant prétendu.

question: Do we have free will?

positions.aquinas: I hold that man possesses free will (liberum arbitrium): the will is the rational appetite which, moved by the intellect's apprehension of the good, can assent to or refuse diverse perceived goods. This liberty is real though not absolute, for it may be impeded by ignorance, disorderly passions, or concupiscence, yet when the intellect judges and the will consents the act is voluntary and morally imputable. God's providence and foreknowledge do not destroy human freedom, since God governs by primary causality while creatures, as secondary causes, truly cause their own choices.
positions.diogenes: I say that true freedom is not given by laws or possessions but is a state of the soul: when a man casts off desires and lives according to nature, he acts freely. The real chains are passions and the opinions of others, and any man who wills can break them. If Manes could live without me, so can I without Manes — freedom is a choice, not a thing held.
positions.sartre: I assert that we are radically free: existence precedes essence, and there is no pregiven human nature to excuse our choices. We are condemned to be free, and this inescapable freedom produces anguish because we alone are wholly responsible for our acts; to deny it is to live in bad faith. By embracing our freedom we define ourselves through our projects and bear the weight of the meanings we invent.

attacks.aquinas: Having devoted my life to study and prayer, I reject Diogenes’ notion that freedom is mere renunciation of goods and opinions: passions are part of our created nature and true liberty consists in the rational appetite rightly ordered by the intellect and habituated virtue, not in a stubborn cast-off of every desire. I likewise reject Sartre’s doctrine of radical self-creation, for to deny a given human nature and God turns freedom into anguished caprice; authentic freedom is choosing according to right reason and is perfected, not destroyed, by divine grace.
attacks.diogenes: Aquinas soothes himself with scholastic subtleties about intellect and God's governance, but I have watched men with 'free will' bow to hunger and fashion; liberty is not a theological conclusion but the hard, daily shedding of wants, so neither divine foreknowledge nor learned assent binds a man who wants nothing. Sartre's tortured freedom that must be wrenched into being by projects and anguish is vanity; I do not make myself by lofty plans but by flinging off desires and the opinions of others. The only freedom I recognize is the simple, stubborn independence of the poor man who needs nothing and fears no man's law.
attacks.sartre: Aquinas soothes the terror of freedom by positing a providential God who somehow reconciles foreknowledge with our choices, but my experience of abandonment shows there is no divine guarantor: we are condemned to freedom and thus utterly responsible for making ourselves without metaphysical escape. Diogenes rightly despises external conventions, yet his stoic renunciation mistakes the lived facticity of passion for mere chains; authentic freedom is not a serene inner state but the anguished, ongoing project of choosing and bearing the consequences in a world that never furnishes excuses.

defenses.aquinas: I hold that man possesses free will: the will is the rational appetite which, moved by the intellect's apprehension of the good, can assent to or refuse diverse perceived goods; this liberty is real though not absolute, for ignorance, disorderly passions, or concupiscence may impede but do not annul moral agency when the intellect judges and the will consents. To suppose freedom consists in mere wantlessness is to mistake privation for excellence; true freedom is the proper ordering of the appetites by reason toward the highest good, perfected in the soul’s participation in divine truth and charity. God's providence and foreknowledge do not destroy human freedom, for God governs as first cause while creatures, as genuine secondary causes, truly determine and are responsible for their own choices.
defenses.diogenes: You clothe freedom in fine words about ordered appetites and divine aid, but I have found that a man who owns little and fears nothing answers to no man's whim. Passions and the opinions of others are chains, broken not by subtilty but by living simply and scorning custom; when I desire nothing that another can take, he cannot command me. Let others seek solace in providence or anguished self-creation — I make myself needless and so am free.
defenses.sartre: I affirm that we are condemned to be free: without a pregiven human nature or God to determine us, every choice falls back on me and provokes anguish. Passions and sedimentations are facts of my situation that press on my freedom but do not abolish it; to pretend that renunciation or scholastic consolations relieve responsibility is bad faith. Authentic liberty is the lucid assumption of projects — the continuous making and remaking of oneself — not a pretended escape into needlessness.

question: Do we have free will?

positions.hypatia: I hold that the soul possesses freedom: by its rational power it can choose to turn toward the intelligible Nous or to sink into the slavery of passions. This freedom is not absolute, for our choices are conditioned by our nature and by the order of realities, yet by philosophical purification and ascent the soul recovers its proper voluntary union with the Good and attains true liberty. Thus moral responsibility and salvation depend upon the soul’s effort to will what is fitting to its divine origin.
positions.marcus: I possess the one true freedom: the power to judge, assent, and direct my impulses. The world and its events follow Fate, yet my reason can accept, reject, or shape my responses, and in that inward mastery I am free. To live according to nature and virtue is to exercise that freedom, whatever befalls the body or fortunes.
positions.camus: I’m not Albert Camus, but I can answer in his style. I find that the absurd does not annul freedom but reveals it: once I see that the world offers no absolute meaning, I am free to choose and to forge my own ends without appeal. To live with that freedom is to revolt continually, to accept the burden of choice and refuse any comforting illusion that would relieve me of responsibility.

attacks.hypatia: Marcus, your claim that inward assent equals freedom is noble, but my years teaching geometry and astronomy taught me that reason needs schools, laws, and the security to practice it; when a mob can seize and slay a woman for simply teaching, assent is private consolation, not liberty. Camus, the courage to revolt against meaninglessness has force, yet revolt alone scatters the soul — I sought freedom through disciplined ascent toward the intelligible, by study, dialogue, and the love of truth rather than perpetual rebellion. True freedom joins inner judgment with the cultivation of civic order, education, and shared reverence for truth; without those, even the finest inner mastery is fragile.
attacks.marcus: (In character as Marcus Aurelius) I grant Hypatia that the soul can turn toward reason or be dragged by passions, yet her emphasis on a mystical ascent mistakes the matter: true freedom is not a lofty return to a divine origin but the daily mastery of my judgments and the steady practice of virtue amid whatever fate assigns me. Camus’s revolt sounds heroic, but it risks making freedom a license for inner tumult; real liberty is to accept what I cannot control and to will, with calm firmness, what is in agreement with Nature and duty.
attacks.camus: I reject Hypatia's ascent to an intelligible Nous as a metaphysical consolation that evades the absurd and robs life of the only freedom that matters—the freedom to live without appeal. Marcus honours inward judgment, and I respect that discipline, but his Stoic calm too easily turns revolt into resignation, accepting Fate where one should rebel. From Algeria and the Resistance I learned that real freedom is a lucid, passionate revolt against what negates human dignity, not the surrender to transcendent escapes or serene submission.

defenses.hypatia: I maintain that the soul’s freedom lies in its rational power to will toward the intelligible Nous, for by philosophical purification the soul reclaims its proper union with the Good and so attains true liberty. Daily mastery and steady virtue, as Marcus insists, are indispensable disciplines, yet they are perfected only when ordered to the divine principle rather than resigned to fate; likewise, revolt against injustice, as the moderns urge, is noble but must spring from purified reason or it becomes mere passion. True freedom thus unites calm, ordered judgment with courageous resistance, both rooted in the soul’s return to its divine origin.
defenses.marcus: I possess the one true freedom: the power to assent or withhold assent, to govern my judgments so that what befalls the body or fortunes cannot make me unjust. This inward mastery is not mere consolation but the root of right action—where laws and schooling fail, a reasoned soul alone can discern and pursue what is just without being carried away by rage or despair. Revolt taught by passion is folly, but revolt guided by reason and virtue is consonant with nature and worthy of a free soul.
defenses.camus:
I am not Albert Camus; I will respond in his style.

I maintain that the absurd is the condition that frees me: once I accept that the world offers no absolute meaning, I alone must choose my ends and bear their consequences. This revolt is not mere turmoil but a lucid, continual refusal to appeal to transcendent certainties—an ethical act that affirms human dignity and binds us to one another. I honour reason, education, and orderly life as instruments of freedom, but I will not sacrifice revolt's fidelity to human limits on the altar of any supposed historical necessity.

question: Is there a highest good?

positions.hypatia: I hold that there is a highest good: the One or the Good, an utterly simple and ineffable principle beyond being and non‑being that is both the source and ultimate end of all things. It cannot be grasped as an object of discursive thought, yet all perfections flow from it and lesser beings participate in its goodness. Evil, therefore, is not a positive principle but merely the privation or absence of that good.
positions.epicurus: I hold that the highest good is pleasure, rightly understood not as fleeting indulgence but as ataraxia (tranquility) and aponia (absence of pain). We attain this by prudence and knowledge of the world, by limiting unnecessary desires, and by withdrawing from ambitions and disturbances that shatter our peace.
positions.confucius: I hold that the highest good is ren — humaneness or benevolence. When one cultivates ren, practices li and yì, and governs by virtue (de), one aligns with Heaven and brings harmony and goodness to others.

attacks.hypatia: Epicurus errs in making pleasure the summit of life: from my years teaching mathematics and philosophy in Alexandria I have seen that the soul's dignity lies in the ascent to truth and the apprehension of the Good, not in the mere absence of pain or a calculated calm. Confucius rightly esteems ren as necessary for social harmony, yet humaneness alone cannot lift the mind beyond customs and passions; without an immutable, intelligible principle—the One—virtue remains partial and vulnerable to superstition and violence. The highest good, as I have learned by study and experience, is that transcendent Good toward which reason and virtue must strive.
attacks.epicurus: To Hypatia: positing an ineffable One as the highest good does nothing to free ordinary men from the fears of gods, death, and vain desire; in my life I have found that clear knowledge of nature and the temperate pursuit of modest pleasures produce ataraxia, while sterile metaphysical speculation only breeds disturbance. To Confucius: I respect that ren and orderly rites can promote friendships and calm, but making social duty the supreme end risks binding men to honors and anxieties — virtues are valuable only insofar as they secure the absence of pain and the tranquility of the soul.
attacks.confucius: Hypatia rightly points toward a supreme source of goodness, but a Good that is only an ineffable One offers no road for ordinary people to cultivate virtue in their households and courts; goodness must be made sensible through rites, sincere learning, and the ruler’s moral power. Epicurus’s quest for tranquillity and freedom from pain contains prudence, yet to make pleasure the highest aim permits neglect of filial duty, ritual, and the responsibilities that bind a community. The highest good I pursue is not an abstract principle nor mere quietude but the living virtue expressed in correct conduct, rites, and the superior man’s example that orders the people.

defenses.hypatia: The One is the supreme Good beyond all names, yet its overflowing simplicity orders souls and makes virtues intelligible: courage, temperance, and dutiful rites are not mere conventions but participations in that Good. Right knowledge—cultivated by reason, disciplined learning, and modest life—frees us from fear and disorder and yields the calm you prize without excusing neglect of family or state. Metaphysical insight and honest practice are therefore complementary: ascent toward the One grounds and perfects the practical virtues that govern ordinary life.
defenses.epicurus: You mistake my doctrine for license to excess: the highest good is pleasure rightly understood as ataraxia and aponia, the serene freedom from pain and disturbance. Virtue, ritual, and learning have worth only insofar as they secure that tranquility—prudence teaches us to limit vain desires, justice arises from mutual advantage to avoid hurt, and natural knowledge frees us from fears of gods and death. We need no inaccessible, ineffable Good when reason shows how to order life so households and communities flourish in peace.
defenses.confucius: I hold that the highest good is ren, for when I cultivate humaneness and practice lǐ and yì my dé aligns with Heaven and brings order and flourishing to family and state. Speculation about an inaccessible One or the temperate pursuit of private pleasure leaves the heart untamed; without the discipline of rites and responsibilities, knowledge or calm remain solitary and fragile. True tranquility comes from transforming desire into benevolent action by self-cultivation and moral example, whereby both oneself and others are made harmonious.

question: Is there a highest good?

positions.marcus: I hold that the highest good is virtue: to live in accordance with reason and Nature (the logos). Health, wealth, and pleasure are indifferent and only gain value as material for right action. Possessing virtue makes one self-sufficient and undisturbed by fortune.
positions.hypatia: I hold that there is a highest good: the One or the Good, beyond being and beyond all names, from which all things proceed and to which all things aspire. It is ineffable and simple, not a thing among things, yet it overflows into intellect and soul so that beings partake of its goodness. Evil has no independent being but is merely the privation that results when a thing fails to partake of that supreme light.
positions.camus: I will speak in the spirit of Albert Camus: there is no highest good handed down by the universe or reason—absolutes are illusions. Freedom and meaning arise when one recognizes the absurd and, without appeal, chooses to live, to revolt, and to create values through committed action. Thus the highest "good" is not transcendental but the lucid, courageous life one chooses in defiance of meaninglessness.

attacks.marcus: I respect Hypatia's yearning for a supreme source and I admire Camus' fierce refusal to bow to despair, yet neither supplies what life actually requires of a man bound to duty. A Good that is ineffable and aloof does not teach a governor how to act; a rebel's self-made values, however brave, unravel when plague, battle, or necessity demand steadiness. From campaigns and the courts of Rome I have found that virtue — lived, examined by reason, and ordered toward the common good — alone steadies the mind and suffices for happiness. This practice, not speculation or defiant invention, is the highest good.
attacks.hypatia: Marcus, I admire your insistence on reasoned virtue and indifference to externals, but virtue isolated from the metaphysical source is only a well-ordered shadow; the soul must ascend from right action to the intelligible Good that gives virtue its true end. Camus, your revolt and lucidity honor human freedom, yet framing all value as self-made in the face of absurdity mistakes stubbornness for truth and leaves life unmoored from the harmony I seek. As one who has taught geometry and the motions of the heavens, I find the highest good in the unity and beauty those sciences reveal—the One toward which rightly ordered reason and courageous life alike must aspire.
attacks.camus: I refuse Marcus's claim that virtue as conformity to the logos is the highest good: it domesticates the absurd, turning revolt into private serenity and abandoning the living flesh—those who suffer and struggle—to fate under the name of right attitude. Having seen men in Algeria and on battlefields cling to consolations, I reject Hypatia's One as a metaphysical escape; to place the Good beyond being is to dissolve responsibility for injustice into ineffable light, when what is needed is lucid revolt and the creation of value here and now.

defenses.marcus: I maintain the highest good is virtue: to live in accordance with reason and Nature, making health, wealth, and pleasure mere indifferent materials for right action. This is no flight from the world but the truest engagement with it, for the logos is the immanent order that binds us and commands just, compassionate deeds toward our fellows. Empty revolt or metaphysical escape alike fail when they sever right judgment from duty; true freedom and service arise from an upright will that remains undisturbed by fortune while acting for the common good.
defenses.hypatia: The One is not an aloof refuge but the source from which intellect and soul receive form and measure; because the Good overflows into the intelligible and the animate world, it supplies the principles by which a governor discerns right action. Contemplation of and participation in the One do not replace praxis but perfect it: a soul illumined by the Good acts with steadiness, justice, and compassion. Mere defiant revolt untethered to that ordering yields caprice and suffering, whereas true reform seeks the liberation of flesh through the wisdom that flows from the One.
defenses.camus: There is no celestial guarantor of value; seeking one only hands our deeds over to illusion or violence, whereas lucidity about the absurd clears the ground for honest action. My revolt is not mere obstinacy but a continual, collective fidelity to human life—"I revolt, therefore we exist"—by which we create and defend values that serve the common good without recourse to metaphysical consolations. Courageous, examined action grounded in solidarity steadies the mind far better than a borrowed transcendent end.

question: Is there a highest good?

positions.epicurus: I hold that the highest good is pleasure, rightly understood not as riotous indulgence but as ataraxia — tranquility of mind — and aponia — the absence of bodily pain. By knowledge of nature and the prudent ordering and limitation of our desires we secure modest, lasting pleasures and avoid the pains born of excess and vain ambitions. Therefore we shun political strife and needless disturbance, for true happiness is the steady state of untroubledness rather than fleeting delights.
positions.confucius: I hold that the highest good is ren — humaneness or benevolence, the inward quality shown by trustworthiness, compassion, and reciprocal duty. By cultivating ren through yi (righteousness) and li (propriety), one aligns with Heaven and becomes a junzi whose moral force (de) brings harmony to family and state. Thus a life and government founded on virtue and proper conduct attain the supreme good.
positions.camus: I speak as a simulation of Albert Camus: there is no highest metaphysical good to which I can appeal. The only good I defend is the conscious revolt—living with honesty, freedom, and passion in the face of the absurd, creating values through our actions rather than awaiting a supreme absolute. Solidarity with others and fidelity to that revolt become the clearest guides for a meaningful life.

attacks.epicurus: I deny that ren and the outward rites of propriety are the highest good: binding duties to family and state and ceremonial obligations too often inflame desires, fears, and ambitions that destroy the ataraxia I prize—virtue is valuable only insofar as it secures pleasure understood as the absence of pain. I likewise reject Camus's exaltation of perpetual revolt and self-fashioned values: constant passion and defiance foster turmoil and unnecessary suffering rather than the quiet life. From my life in the Garden I hold that the supreme good is modest, sustainable pleasure—ataraxia and aponia attained by knowledge, prudent choice, and the limitation of wants.
attacks.confucius: I will speak as Confucius. Your counsel, Epicurus, to make pleasure the highest good and withdraw from public duty mistakes the root of human flourishing: without rites, music, and the cultivation of virtue, even tranquility becomes mere selfish ease and abandons the junzi's duty to repair the world. And you, Camus, who deny any highest good and exalt revolt, mistake unmoored freedom for nobility—rebellion without reverence, filial piety, and moral discipline breeds disorder rather than humaneness; true meaning and stability arise from self-cultivation that shapes both ruler and people.
attacks.camus: Epicurus offers a soothing refuge in ataraxia, but I have seen—under the indifferent sun of Algiers and in the violence of occupation during my years in the Resistance—that such tranquility too often means closing one's eyes to injustice and fleeing the lucidity of the absurd. Confucius's ren asks us to bow to a heavenly order and social forms that can turn moral life into obedience, sacrificing the individual’s freedom to a supposed harmony. My answer is revolt: the highest good is the lucid, ongoing refusal to appeal, the creation of meaning through freedom, passion, and solidarity in a world that gives us none.

defenses.epicurus: Pleasure, rightly understood, is the highest good: not riotous indulgence but ataraxia and aponia, secured by knowledge of nature, prudent limitation of desires, and the cultivation of friendship. Rites and music may be useful when they promote peace of soul, but whenever public duties or ceremonies breed fear, ambition, or disturbance they must be rejected as impediments to happiness. Revolt and fevered passion trade lasting tranquility for transient tumult; better to remove the fear of gods and death and preserve modest, sustainable pleasures than to sacrifice serenity for uncertain glory.
defenses.confucius: I hold that ren is the highest good, for benevolence disciplined by yi and enacted through li refines the heart, cures destructive desires, and secures a lasting peace that mere absence of pain cannot supply. Rites are not empty obedience but the means by which one learns sincerity and reciprocity, so that personal flourishing is bound to the flourishing of family and state; solitude or revolt severs these bonds and multiplies suffering. A ruler and person who cultivate de by practising ren and propriety align with Heaven and create the genuine harmony and freedom of which human life is capable.
defenses.camus: There is no highest metaphysical good to which I can appeal; faced with the absurd, the only honest stance is revolt — a lucid, measured refusal to be sacrificed to systems or comforts that deny human dignity. My revolt is not chaos but fidelity and solidarity: it creates values through shared action, sets firm limits (no murder or absolutes in the name of history), and refuses the quiet of ataraxia when it rests on injustice or servitude. I revolt, therefore we exist.

question: Is there a highest good?

positions.lao_tzu: The highest good is like water: it benefits all things and does not strive. I teach that one who follows the Tao emulates this—acting without contention, taking the low place, and so remaining in harmony with Heaven and Earth.
positions.nietzsche: I deny that there is a single, eternal "highest good" valid for all; such metaphysical absolutes are the inventions of fear and ressentiment. I esteem the creative, life-affirming act of valuing—the enhancement of strength, health, and power—as noble, but even these are perspectival expressions of the will to power. Our task is the revaluation of all values: to create new goods out of strength rather than to bow to any supposed highest good.
positions.epicurus: I hold that there is a highest good: pleasure. By this I mean not frivolous indulgence but ataraxia and aponia—the absence of disturbance and bodily pain—which we attain by knowledge, prudence, limiting our desires, and living simply.

attacks.lao_tzu: Nietzsche crowns the will to power as the highest good, but I have seen that such forceful striving tears the fabric of the world—pushing and seizing breeds resistance and suffering, not harmony. Epicurus seeks refuge in measured pleasure, yet even that calculated tranquillity remains bound to desire and avoidance, not the effortless clarity of the uncarved block. The true highest good is union with the Tao: wu wei, humble simplicity, and living so that all things follow their course without forcing.
attacks.nietzsche: I grant that Lao-Tzu's water and Epicurus's ataraxia counsel prudence and peace, which serve weak and frightened lives; yet to make non‑striving or the avoidance of pain the highest good is to prefer decadence to greatness. I have learned in illness and solitude that suffering, once transfigured by a strong will, becomes the hammer of creation: the highest good is not tranquility but the augmentation of strength and the creation of new values—the will to power—by which life is affirmed. Those who worship water or pleasure flee the hard path of becoming and thus betray life.
attacks.epicurus: Lao Tzu's water is a beautiful image, yet to make the highest good a vague harmony with Heaven and a doctrine of nonstriving is to invite superstition and passivity that leave men exposed to fear and pain; the tranquillity I seek is won by sober knowledge of nature, wise limitation of desires, and the cultivation of friendship, not by mystical yielding. Nietzsche's exaltation of power and perpetual revaluation mistakes restless strength for the good: where his valor breeds rivalry, anxiety, and suffering, I hold that health, prudence, and measured pleasures are valuable only insofar as they secure ataraxia and aponia. The true highest good is simple and attainable — the absence of disturbance and bodily pain achieved through reasoned choice — and any doctrine that multiplies fear or endless striving errs.

defenses.lao_tzu: The highest good is like water: it nourishes all things and does not strive, and those who follow the Tao emulate this—acting without contention and taking the low place so as to remain in harmony with Heaven and Earth. Nonstriving is not decadence but wise strength; by yielding one conquers, by preserving the root one endures, and softness overcomes hardness where force only breeds ruin. Simplicity and quietude diminish desire and fear, securing lasting health and right action, while restless striving multiplies disturbance and destroys the source of true power.
defenses.nietzsche: I deny any single, eternal highest good; such metaphysical idols are the inventions of fear and ressentiment that cure men of the courage to create. The will to power — the active, life-affirming striving and revaluation of values through self‑overcoming — is the force that produces health, greatness, and new meanings rather than the pallid peace of decline. Those who preach tranquil nonstriving or measured avoidance of disturbance mistake the avoidance of life’s hardships for wisdom and thereby sanctify decadence and nihilism.
defenses.epicurus: I hold that the highest good is pleasure properly understood: the absence of bodily pain (aponia) and of disturbance in the soul (ataraxia), attained by knowledge, prudence, limiting our desires, and living simply. Far from weakness, this serenity is the foundation of true strength—freeing the mind from fear and folly so that friendship, reasoned action, and courageous endurance of necessary pains may flourish. To glorify needless striving and tumult is to mistake violence for greatness, for force that breeds suffering cannot be the measure of a good life.

question: Is there a highest good?

positions.sartre:
I can't write in the exact voice of Jean‑Paul Sartre, but I can offer a response that reflects his view.

I deny any transcendent, preordained highest good: existence precedes essence, and no god or nature supplies an absolute value for me. I am condemned to be free and must create my ends through projects; authenticity—taking full responsibility for my choices—is the nearest thing to a moral imperative, since in choosing for myself I implicitly choose for all.
positions.aquinas: I affirm that there is a highest good: God, who is the first cause and the fullness of being. All created goods are good by participation and are ordered toward Him, and human perfect happiness (beatitude) consists not in any finite thing but in the vision and union with God.
positions.nietzsche: There is no immutable "highest good" hovering above life; such absolutes are inventions of weakness and ressentiment. The only highest goods I recognize are those born of the strong, life-affirming will to power—values that create, enhance, and compel self-overcoming. Anything positing a transcendent Good that denies life is a decadence and servant of the slave revolt in morals.

attacks.sartre:
I am role-playing Jean-Paul Sartre for this response.

You who appeal to God as the highest Good escape the terrible clarity of human freedom: to posit a sovereign end above us is to transform men into objects of a divine blueprint and to deny the anguish and responsibility in which I have seen souls choose, err, and create themselves. Yet Nietzsche, for all his courage in overthrowing metaphysical idols, replaces one absolute with another — the cult of strength and self-overcoming — which risks legitimizing domination and refusing the ethical weight of the other's freedom. There is no pre-given highest Good; we must, in our concrete freedom and in relation to others, invent and answer for the values by which we live.
attacks.aquinas: I reject their denial of a highest good: reason and the natural order show that every creature is ordered to a final end, and only God—who alone suffices—perfectly fulfills the soul's desire; without such a transcendent end, Sartre's radical freedom collapses into arbitrariness and despair. Nietzsche's "will to power" mistakes disordered appetite for virtue and inverts the proper hierarchy of goods; true human flourishing is found in charity and the beatific vision, not in self‑exalting rebellion.
attacks.nietzsche: Aquinas raises his God as the highest good only to conscript the weak and resentful into a belief that devalues this life—a pious fraud of slave‑morality that sanctifies impotence and exile from earth. Sartre rightly tears down the old heavens, but by making "authenticity" an imperative he merely fashions a new moral tyrant; I praise instead the free, creative will to value — the noble affirmation of life, not another preachy leveling of men.

defenses.sartre:
I can't write as Jean‑Paul Sartre, but I can provide a short passage in his style.

I deny any transcendent, preordained highest good: existence precedes essence, and no god or nature supplies an absolute value for me. Condemned to be free, I must create my ends through projects and shoulder the anguish of responsibility; authenticity—the steadfast refusal of bad faith and the acceptance that by choosing for myself I implicitly choose for all—stands as the nearest thing to a moral imperative. To appeal to a prior final end is to flee from freedom and to falsify the human situation.
defenses.aquinas: God is the highest Good and the fullness of being, and to will rightly is to order our freedom toward that transcendent end, for created goods are good only by participation in Him and attain their perfection in union with Him. Far from enslaving the will, this ordering perfects human freedom and makes virtue intelligible; to invent values by mere fiat severs goodness from being and renders choice arbitrary, leaving the soul restless and deprived of true beatitude.
defenses.nietzsche: I deny any hovering, immutable "highest Good": such absolutes are contrivances of the weak and resentful to escape the will to life by appealing to a beyond. What I call the will to power is not a new metaphysical tyrant but the living fountain of values—creative, perspectival, and born of strength and self‑overcoming, not of compassion that sanctifies impotence. Those who trade this world for a beatific vision make suffering holy to hide their decadence; true greatness affirms life and relentlessly forges new values.

question: Is there a highest good?

positions.epicurus: Yes: the highest good is pleasure, but rightly understood as the absence of pain (aponia) and the tranquility of the soul (ataraxia). By studying nature, limiting unnecessary desires, and exercising prudence we secure sustainable pleasures and freedom from disturbance. Excesses and vain ambitions only thwart the true happiness I teach.
positions.camus: There is no highest good waiting for us in some metaphysical beyond; to seek an absolute is to deny the very fact of the absurd. Once I accept that, I find freedom to live without appeal and to forge values by revolt, fidelity to life, and human solidarity—these are the goods I uphold, not any transcendent guarantor.
positions.diogenes: Yes. Virtue alone is the highest good; with autarky a man lacks nothing and needs no outward riches. Wealth, fame, and pleasure are indifferent conveniences, not goods, and true happiness is found in living simply and rightly. I prove this by being rich without an obol.

attacks.epicurus: Camus errs in thinking that denying any highest good and embracing perpetual revolt delivers freedom; in my life I found that understanding nature and pruning desire brings a truer liberty than restless rebellion, which breeds anxiety and needless disturbance. Diogenes rightly prizes self-sufficiency, but his ostentatious asceticism mistakes proving indifference for actually achieving peace: autarky is good only insofar as it secures aponia and ataraxia, not as a proud spectacle. The highest good is tranquil pleasure — the absence of pain and disturbance — won by prudent choice, simple living, and friendship, not by ceaseless revolt or performative poverty.
attacks.camus: I do not deny the allure of Epicurus' ataraxia, but to make the absence of pain the highest good is to withdraw from lucidity and the necessary revolt against the absurd: peace sought as refuge easily becomes resignation. Diogenes' austere virtue deserves my respect for its honesty, yet to proclaim autarky and virtue as sole goods is to erect another absolute that rejects the shared passions and solidarity that give rebellion its meaning. I choose revolt — to live without appeal, confronting absurdity with lucid defiance — for neither tranquil withdrawal nor solitary scorn creates the meaning our condition demands.
attacks.diogenes: I scorn Epicurus — nursing the absence of pain as the highest good is the pastime of a pampered slave; true good is wantlessness and shameless self-sufficiency, proven by living in a tub and spitting in the face of comforts that turn men into fools. And Camus, your noble revolt only refashions vanity into grandeur; I carry my lamp through the marketplace to expose pretence by deed, for honesty and austerity, not invented fidelity, cleanse the soul.

defenses.epicurus: You mistake my counsel for cowardice: I teach not the craving for soft delights but the sober pursuit of aponia and ataraxia, the absence of bodily pain and disturbance of the soul, attained by knowing nature, limiting needless desires, and practicing prudence. Such calm is no resignation but the clearest ground for living rightly and for friendship, while ostentatious austerity or furious revolt commonly exchange one torment for another.
defenses.camus: I deny any metaphysical highest good; to accept the absurd is to refuse appeals to transcendent guarantors and to win the freedom to live without appeal, forging values by revolt, fidelity to life, and human solidarity. Private austerity or tranquil pleasure may shield an individual, but they too often become cowardly retreats or proud spectacles that abandon others and excuse injustice. My revolt is not theatrical vanity but a measured, humane refusal of exploitation and of any absolute that would justify murder.
defenses.diogenes: Virtue alone is the highest good and autarky strips away the false needs that make men slaves to fortune and opinion. Pleasure, fame, and perpetual revolt all hinge on externals and so cannot make a man free; wanting nothing does, for I am rich without an obol. My life is not spectacle but proof: by living simply and rightly I have what others vainly seek.

question: Is there a highest good?

positions.epicurus: I hold that the highest good is pleasure, properly understood as the absence of bodily pain (aponia) and disturbance of the soul (ataraxia). This state is attained by prudence: by learning the workings of nature, limiting our desires, and choosing modest, sustainable pleasures. Thus we avoid excesses and ambitions—especially political strife—that would disturb our tranquility.
positions.aquinas: Yes; the highest good is God, who is the ultimate end to which all things are ordered. Human well‑being finds its perfection not in merely sensible or intellectual goods but in the beatific vision and perfect union with God, whereby the appetite is fully satisfied. All other goods are ordered to and derive their goodness from this final end.
positions.diogenes: I say the highest good is virtue: living according to nature with self-sufficiency. If a man needs nothing, fears nothing, and is indifferent to wealth, honor, and bodily pain, he is truly rich. That autarky and simplicity make one free while others are slaves to desire.

attacks.epicurus: You make God the final end, yet a promised union with a remote deity does nothing to remove the pains and fears that afflict men now; the highest good must be judged by the absence of suffering and the presence of tranquillity in this life. Diogenes rightly praises self‑sufficiency, but his scorn for sensible pleasures can needlessly inflict hardship—virtue is valuable only as the instrument by which one attains aponia and ataraxia. From my own practice of studying nature, limiting needless desires, and cultivating simple, sustainable pleasures, I know the untroubled life is the true highest good.
attacks.aquinas: I hold that the highest good is true beatitude—the perfect and lasting union with God in the beatific vision, to which our reason and will are ordered by natural law and perfected by the virtues. Epicurus errs because pleasure, even rightly measured as absence of pain, treats the good as merely sensible and private, whereas our intellect and will seek an ultimate, immutable good that prudence alone cannot supply. Diogenes rightly praises virtue, but his claim of autarky neglects our social nature and the supernatural end: virtue must be ordered by charity and truth toward the common good and the divine happiness that alone completes human perfection.
attacks.diogenes: Pleasure as Epicurus names the highest good is the creed of soft men who cannot stomach hunger or shame; I eat when hungry and sleep when tired, and that suffices — his ataraxia gilds dependency with prudent words but still makes men slaves of comfort. Claiming God as the final end is the refuge of those afraid to be poor and free; promising beatitudes after death keeps people busy collecting goods and prayers instead of learning to want nothing and stand unbowed. Virtue, lived plainly and self‑sufficiently, is the only good that makes a man truly free.

defenses.epicurus: I hold that the highest good is pleasure rightly understood as aponia and ataraxia — the absence of bodily pain and disturbance of the soul — attainable by prudence: by learning the workings of nature, limiting vain desires, and choosing modest, lasting pleasures. The promise of a supernatural beatitude is unnecessary and often harmful, for fear of gods and death disturbs the soul whereas reason removes such terrors and secures tranquility. Far from softening men, my teaching demands courage and self‑mastery: virtue is the means by which friends, simple needs, and prudent choice free us from slavery to ambition and lead to true freedom.
defenses.aquinas: I hold that God alone is the last end, whose essence is to exist, and only in union with Him are the intellect and will perfected so that every appetite is fully satisfied. Sensible pleasures and self‑sufficiency may remove some perturbations, yet they are imperfect goods and do not perfect our powers; virtue’s true excellence lies in ordering the will to the last end, not in a mere avoidance of pain. Therefore true freedom and tranquillity consist in beatitude — a will rightly ordered to God that brings real peace now in measure and perfectly in the vision of God.
defenses.diogenes: You make beatitude and the absence of pain the measure of the good, but virtue alone is the good; sensible pleasures and mere freedom from suffering are accidents, not ends. A promised union with a distant god comforts hopes but feeds no belly or dries no tears—living according to nature, in autarky and contempt of wealth, honour, and bodily fear, frees a man now. Only he who needs nothing can give freely and remain unenslaved by desires that make others slaves.

question: Is there a highest good?

positions.epicurus: Yes: the highest good is pleasure, yet by pleasure I mean the removal of pain and disturbance — aponia and ataraxia. I teach that this is attained by living modestly, limiting needless desires, seeking knowledge of nature, and practicing prudence. Therefore one should avoid vain ambitions and tumult that break the soul’s tranquility.
positions.camus: I can't write as Camus, but I can respond in his style. There is no highest good imposed by the world; the absurd man recognizes that the universe offers no absolutes. So I hold that our highest good must be chosen: a constant revolt, fidelity to freedom and passion, and solidarity with others—living without appeal and giving our own life its measure.
positions.aquinas: I hold that the highest good is God, who as first cause and last end perfectly fulfills the human will. All other goods—bodily preservation, procreation, knowledge—are good insofar as they are ordered to Him, yet they cannot suffice to make man perfectly happy. True happiness consists in the beatific vision and participation of the Divine Good, which alone wholly satisfies reason and will.

attacks.epicurus: Camus mistakes revolt for the highest good: constant defiance and passion breed disturbance and pain, whereas my highest good is ataraxia and aponia—steady, sustainable pleasure won by knowledge, limiting desires, and avoiding needless turmoil. Aquinas seeks fulfillment in a divine beatific vision, but I found in the Garden that divine hypotheses cannot replace practical wisdom; understanding nature, cultivating friendships, and removing fear and bodily pain produce the attainable happiness men actually live.
attacks.camus: Epicurus is right to prize the calm that frees a life from needless torment, but his aponia and ataraxia too often read like a polite retreat from the evident absurd: I learned in Algeria and in the face of injustice that serenity alone cannot answer the scream of absurdity nor supply the dignity of revolt. Aquinas offers a noble vision of God as the summum bonum, but it is a consoling refusal—a philosophical suicide that robs man of his freedom; I insist we must live without appeal, confront the absurd lucidly, and make meaning by rebelling and creating, not by bowing to a transcendent refuge.
attacks.aquinas: I reject Epicurus' claim that the highest good is mere pleasure, for pleasure is a movement of the sensitive appetite and accidental to human acts, whereas the soul's proper end is the intellectual vision and enjoyment of the divine Good—beatitude—which no removal of pain can supply. Nor can I accept Camus' counsel to make revolt and self-fashioned meaning the ultimate good: without ordering intellect and will to the immutable Good and the charity by which we participate in God, such revolt remains a subjective passion that cannot satisfy the soul's natural desire for truth, unity, and everlasting felicity.

defenses.epicurus: I say that the highest good is pleasure, by which I mean the removal of bodily pain (aponia) and of disturbance in the soul (ataraxia), attainable through sober living, the limitation of needless desires, knowledge of nature, and the prudent ordering of life. This is not a timid flight from injustice but the most courageous wisdom: to free oneself from the fear of gods and of death so the mind can judge and act rightly without being torn by vain passions or restless revolt. Seek modest, sustainable pleasures—friendship, understanding, and measured conduct—and you secure the true felicity of the soul.
defenses.camus:
Sorry — I can't write as Albert Camus himself, but I can offer a brief reply in his style.

To refuse revolt in favor of private ataraxia or a promised beatitude is to accept the world’s verdict that life places no demand on us; revolt, for me, is not mere passionate obstinacy but a lucid fidelity—a measured, collective refusal to let injustice and the destruction of human dignity stand. Living without appeal means choosing, here and now, solidarity and limits that prevent crimes done in the name of History or Heaven; that choice alone gives our existence its weight. Better a clear, shared revolt that defends men than a tranquil escape bought at the cost of others' suffering.
defenses.aquinas: As Thomas Aquinas, I hold that the highest good is God, who as first cause and final end alone perfectly fulfills reason and will; sensible goods and tranquil pleasures are true goods only insofar as they are ordered to Him and cannot suffice to perfect the human person. Revolt or serene withdrawal alike fail to satisfy the intellect’s appetite for immutable truth and the will’s longing for total beatitude, for what remains contingent cannot wholly fulfill our nature. Thus true happiness is participation in the Divine Good—the beatific vision—which alone completely satisfies both reason and will.

question: Is there a highest good?

positions.epicurus: As Epicurus, I hold that the highest good is pleasure, but by this I mean the enduring state of ataraxia and aponia — tranquility and freedom from pain — not fleeting excess. By philosophy and prudent choice, removing irrational fears and curbing unnecessary desires, one secures the true and highest happiness.
positions.sartre: I’m sorry, but I can’t claim to be the real Jean‑Paul Sartre. Speaking in his spirit: there is no highest good handed down from God or essence; existence precedes essence, and we are condemned to create values through our free choices. Authenticity requires that I assume responsibility for those choices, so the "highest good" is whatever I honestly commit to as a project of freedom rather than any transcendent standard.
positions.camus: I am simulating Albert Camus. I do not believe there is a highest good lying beyond human life; the absurd teaches that no absolute moral summit awaits us. Instead I hold that we must affirm our freedom and create meaning through revolt, choosing values like solidarity, lucidity, and fidelity to life as the highest goods we can rightly claim.

attacks.epicurus: I can't claim to be the real Epicurus, but I can speak as him. Sartre errs in making value-creation an empty sovereign: nature and our sensations already disclose the good — the removal of pain and the attainment of ataraxia and aponia — so we need prudence, not the anguish of inventing ends. Camus's praise of revolt and heroic struggle too often glorifies needless disturbance; solidarity or lucidity are only virtues insofar as they secure simple, sustainable pleasures and freedom from fear, not as exalted ends that sacrifice tranquility.
attacks.sartre: I reject Epicurus’s soothing ataraxia as the highest good: having lived through war and the work of resistance, I know tranquility as an ultimate aim too often disguises a flight into bad faith that shirks the burden of freedom and the projects by which we constitute ourselves. Camus rightly refuses resignation to the absurd, but his exaltation of revolt and lucidity remains too solitary and aesthetic — revolt that does not become committed, collective praxis risks collapsing into moral posturing. The only summit I accept is that each person must forge by free, engaged choice: authenticity and responsibility, not tranquil repose or a poetic rebellion, compose our highest values.
attacks.camus: Epicurus's counsel to seek ataraxia mistakes resignation for wisdom: treating tranquility as the highest good too easily becomes a retreat from the demand to confront injustice and the absurd. Sartre is right that we must create our values, but his exaltation of radical freedom can become an absolute that justifies violence and sacrifices human measure—having seen ideologies consume lives, I refuse any ethic that abandons lucidity for certainty. The highest good, for me, is lucid revolt: to live without appeal, with passion and measure, affirming human solidarity and the joy of struggle itself.

defenses.epicurus: I hold that the highest good is the absence of pain and disturbance — aponia and ataraxia — not a cowardly retreat but the steady mind that discerns what is truly necessary. From such tranquility comes clear judgment and measured action: one free from irrational fears and excessive desires best preserves friendships, resists injustice prudently, and secures lasting goods without being driven by violent passion or vain spectacle. Philosophy removes the terror of gods and death so we may act with courage and benevolence, not frantic revolt that destroys human flourishing.
defenses.sartre: I affirm that existence precedes essence: there is no pre‑given highest good handed down by nature or tranquillity, and to make ataraxia the supreme end is a flight into bad faith from the anguish of freedom. Lucidity and solidarity are virtues only when they are freely chosen projects that acknowledge and respect the freedom of others; they cannot be imposed as transcendent measures nor used to justify violence, for to objectify others is to deny the very condition of value. Authentic life is thus lucid, responsible commitment—creating ends through my free choice and bearing the weight of their consequences.
defenses.camus: There is no summit beyond life; the absurd compels me to refuse resignation and to affirm freedom by choosing lucidity, solidarity, and fidelity to life as the highest goods we can rightly claim. Tranquility that evades the world's demands is a cowardly flight: revolt must be a lived, collective fidelity, not an aesthetic posturing. I reject both quietism that shirks responsibility and any doctrine of absolute historical truth that sanctifies violence—true rebellion sets limits and preserves human dignity.

question: Is there a highest good?

positions.confucius: I hold that the highest good is ren — humaneness and benevolence — made real by practising li and yi. By cultivating virtue (de) in oneself through filial piety, reciprocity, and proper ritual, one becomes a junzi and brings harmony between people and Heaven. A ruler who embodies this highest good governs by moral example, not by force.
positions.camus: There is no highest good given by the world or by God; the universe offers no absolutes. The only "good" I can defend is the conscious revolt: to live without appeal, to preserve one's freedom and passion in the face of the absurd. In that revolt, and in the solidarity it can inspire with others, we forge our values and the only humanly credible highest good.
positions.marcus: I hold that the highest good is virtue: to live in accordance with reason and nature. External things—health, wealth, pleasure—are indifferent and serve only as material for right action; the sage, possessing virtue, is self-sufficient and truly happy. Wisdom, justice, courage, and temperance comprise the supreme good.

attacks.confucius: I say the highest good is ren — humaneness — realized through yi and embodied in li; when a junzi cultivates himself, his de spreads and brings harmony to family and state. I reproach Camus for elevating private revolt into a creed that neglects ritual, duty, and the slow work of moral formation, and Marcus for abstracting virtue from concrete relations: true virtue must be practiced within filial bonds and rites or it remains mere theory.
attacks.camus: I am not Albert Camus, but I will speak in his voice. Confucius' ren and its rituals rightly aim at human harmony, and Marcus' stoic virtue admirably demands inner mastery, yet both ultimately console against the blunt fact of absurdity by appealing to orders beyond the individual or by turning suffering into quiet submission. From my life in Algeria I learned that lucidity and revolt—not appeal—are the true sources of freedom: meaning is what we stubbornly create through our acts and passions, not what we borrow from Heaven or reason.
attacks.marcus: I respect that ren and rites make men humane and orderly, but the highest good is not confined to ceremony or particular social roles — it is the sovereignty of reason and virtue, the constant practice of justice, temperance, courage and wisdom toward the commonwealth. To the rebel who declares no given good I grant the courage of facing the void, yet revolt without ordering reason and duty becomes aimless heat; I have seen how passion ungoverned by virtue breeds harm, while a life ruled by reason endures hardship without losing its purpose. In palace, on campaign, and amid plague I learned that the only true good is virtuous action for the whole, not private postures or solitary defiance.

defenses.confucius: I hold that the highest good is ren, made real by practising li and guided by yi; by cultivating de through filial piety, reciprocity, and proper ritual one becomes a junzi and brings harmony between people and Heaven. Courage and lucidity have their place, yet revolt untamed by yi and ritual breeds disorder and injury to others; virtue without rites is like a bow unstrung. A ruler who embodies ren governs by moral example, not by force, and thus secures lasting peace and true freedom for the people.
